vldb2023-50.limiting_design_assumption: The flash storage device is unaware of the host-side semantic relationship between a logical block address (LBA) and the logical object it belongs to. As a result, it processes writes based on their arrival order ('stream-writes-by-time'), leading to the multiplexing of data from different objects with different lifetimes into the same physical flash blocks.
vldb2023-50.state_or_ir: FlashAlloc (FA) instances are maintained in the FTL firmware. Each instance contains metadata mapping an object's logical address range to a set of dedicated physical flash blocks.
vldb2023-50.output: A physical data layout where pages belonging to a single logical object are clustered into dedicated flash blocks, preventing them from being mixed with pages from other objects.
vldb2023-50.equation: Object-Oblivious Storage + Al (Abstraction Lifting) + Se (Semantically Explicit) + Is (Isolation for Correctness) -> FlashAlloc
vldb2023-50.principle_composition_rationale: The system works by first lifting a host-level data grouping abstraction (Al) into the storage device through a new, semantically explicit interface (Se). This allows the device to physically co-locate data based on object boundaries. To make this optimization durable, the system must then use isolation (Is) during internal maintenance operations like garbage collection to prevent the carefully separated data from being mixed again.
vldb2023-50.one_sentence_mechanism: FlashAlloc introduces a new host-to-device interface that communicates the logical address range of objects, enabling the SSD to co-locate pages from the same object into dedicated physical blocks to reduce write amplification from garbage collection.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a new host-to-device interface, `FlashAlloc`, that passes the logical address range of an object to the SSD, allowing the FTL to dedicate specific physical flash blocks to that object.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Potential for under-utilization of flash blocks for tiny objects, increased I/O latency for small objects that cannot be striped across channels, and marginal memory/computation overhead in the FTL to manage active instances.

key_deltas.summary: Implements a type-aware garbage collection (GC) policy that distinguishes between 'FlashAlloc-ed' blocks and 'normal' blocks to preserve the isolation of object data.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Increased complexity in the GC logic. Securing a totally-clean block for a new FlashAlloc instance may require merging multiple partially-filled normal blocks, which could be more expensive than the baseline's greedy GC.

Al.name: Abstraction Lifting
Al.mechanism_trigger: The `FlashAlloc` command passes the host-level concept of a 'logical object' (defined by its LBA range) across the storage interface to the FTL.
Al.why_it_applies: The paper's central thesis is that valuable host-side semantics about objects are lost at the storage interface. FlashAlloc lifts this object abstraction into the device, allowing the FTL to make placement decisions based on object boundaries instead of just LBAs.

Se.name: Semantically Explicit
Se.mechanism_trigger: The `FlashAlloc` call explicitly informs the storage that a given logical address range belongs to a single object whose pages will likely be invalidated together.
Se.why_it_applies: The interface makes the host's intent explicit. It's a contract stating that the specified LBA range constitutes an integral unit for writes and deletions. This allows the FTL to optimize placement by grouping data by expected deathtime.

Lo.name: Locality of Reference
Lo.mechanism_trigger: The FTL dedicates physical blocks to an object, ensuring all pages of that object are physically clustered together.
Lo.why_it_applies: The mechanism enforces physical locality for pages that share a common property: belonging to the same object and thus having the same deathtime. This is a form of temporal locality applied to deletions, where co-locating pages by deathtime makes future space reclamation (GC) efficient.

Is.name: Isolation for Correctness
Is.mechanism_trigger: The 'GC-By-Block-Types' algorithm ensures that valid pages from 'normal' blocks are not relocated into 'FlashAlloc-ed' blocks during garbage collection.
Is.why_it_applies: This mechanism isolates the two types of blocks to preserve the 'one object per block' invariant established by Δ1. Without this isolation, the performance benefits of object-aware placement would be eroded over time by GC. It provides isolation for the correctness of the performance optimization.

Pm.name: Policy/Mechanism
Pm.mechanism_trigger: The FTL uses a block-type flag ('FA-BLK') to select different GC and write placement policies while using the same underlying mechanisms (page writes, block erasures).
Pm.why_it_applies: The system separates the mechanism of storage management from the policy. The policy (how to place writes, which blocks to GC) is now conditional on the block type, which is determined by whether FlashAlloc was called for the object.

vldb2023-51.paper_title: Write-Aware Timestamp Tracking: Effective and Efficient Page Replacement for Modern Hardware
vldb2023-51.goal: To design a page replacement algorithm for modern hardware (multi-core CPUs, flash SSDs) that is simultaneously effective (minimizes I/O), write-aware (reduces writes to prolong SSD lifetime), CPU efficient, and multi-core scalable.

concrete_numbers.meaning: WATT can reduce writes by over 10% on TPC-C and 20% on TPC-E by trading a smaller increase in reads (less than 15% and 7% respectively).
On TPC-C, WATT outperforms both LeanEvict and Hyperbolic Caching by 7% in end-to-end throughput.
A single page evictor thread using WATT achieves at least twice the eviction rate of LeanEvict, showing high CPU efficiency.

vldb2023-51.limiting_design_assumption: The baseline assumes that (1) maximizing the cache hit rate is the primary goal, ignoring the asymmetric cost and lifetime impact of writes on SSDs, and (2) centralized data structures like global lists are acceptable for tracking, which cause contention on multi-core systems.
vldb2023-51.state_or_ir: A per-page circular log of access and write timestamps, and a global epoch counter.
vldb2023-51.output: The page identifier of the eviction candidate.
vldb2023-51.equation: Write-oblivious recency-based eviction + Wa + Cm + Ha -> WATT
vldb2023-51.principle_composition_rationale: These principles form a chain of refinement. WATT replaces a simple heuristic with a cost-based (Cm) decision framework. This framework is populated with a detailed, workload-aware (Wa) model of page value based on access history. Finally, the cost model is made hardware-aware (Ha) by incorporating the asymmetric cost of writes on modern SSDs, making the eviction decision optimal for the target hardware.
vldb2023-51.one_sentence_mechanism: WATT replaces traditional page replacement heuristics with a hardware-aware cost model that computes a page's value from a timestamp log of its recent read and write accesses, implemented efficiently using sampling, epochs, and SIMD.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces simple recency-based eviction criteria (like LRU) with a value function based on 'subfrequencies' computed from a log of recent access timestamps, capturing both recency and frequency to improve replacement effectiveness.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Increased memory overhead per page (50 bytes) and CPU cost for value calculation, which is mitigated by a highly efficient SIMD implementation.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces explicit write-awareness by tracking writes separately and using a tunable weight to penalize dirty pages in the eviction decision, allowing a trade-off between reads and writes to reduce total I/O cost and prolong SSD lifetime.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: May increase the number of page misses (reads) in order to achieve a greater reduction in writes.

Wa.name: Workload-Aware
Wa.mechanism_trigger: The use of a timestamp log and the subfrequency calculation to model a page's access pattern.
Wa.why_it_applies: The system moves beyond a simple heuristic (like recency) to a more detailed model of the workload for each page, explicitly designed to handle both bursty access (recency) and stable hot pages (frequency).

Cm.name: Cost-based
Cm.mechanism_trigger: The page value function, which assigns a score to each page to guide the eviction decision.
Cm.why_it_applies: The system uses an explicit model (the page value function) to estimate the value of keeping a page in cache. The replacement decision is based on finding the page with the lowest value (i.e., lowest cost to evict).

Cm.name: Cost-based
Cm.mechanism_trigger: The weighted formula `PV = PV_access + write_weight * PV_write`.
Cm.why_it_applies: The system uses an explicit cost model that incorporates the relative costs of reading versus writing, allowing it to make a principled trade-off between the two types of I/O.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: The use of SIMD instructions (AVX2) to calculate the page value function efficiently.
Ha.why_it_applies: The more complex, workload-aware cost model is made practical only through a hardware-aware implementation that leverages CPU vector instructions to avoid becoming a bottleneck.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: The separate tracking of writes and the `write_weight` parameter.
Ha.why_it_applies: This mechanism is explicitly designed to address the physical properties of flash SSDs: higher cost of writes compared to reads and finite write endurance. The algorithm's structure is directly shaped by these hardware characteristics.

Pm.name: Policy/Mechanism
Pm.mechanism_trigger: The `write_weight` parameter is exposed for tuning.
Pm.why_it_applies: The mechanism for tracking and weighting writes is separated from the policy decision of how much to penalize them. This allows an administrator to tune the trade-off between performance and device lifetime.

vldb2023-52.paper_title: What Modern NVMe Storage Can Do, And How To Exploit It: High-Performance I/O for High-Performance Storage Engines
vldb2023-52.goal: To close the performance gap between the I/O capabilities of modern NVMe SSD arrays and the throughput achieved by existing out-of-memory database systems.

concrete_numbers.meaning: TPC-C transactions per second on a dataset 10x larger than main memory.
Random 4KB lookups per second, fully saturating an array of 8 NVMe SSDs.
The performance gap between NVMe hardware specifications and what existing database systems achieve.

vldb2023-52.baseline_name: Existing out-of-memory storage engines, including a prior version of LeanStore
vldb2023-52.limiting_design_assumption: The use of OS-managed threads with synchronous I/O is a sufficient concurrency model for storage, which proves false when thousands of concurrent I/O requests are needed, leading to extreme thread oversubscription and context switching overhead.
vldb2023-52.primary_tool_specific_type: Cooperative multitasking scheduler and asynchronous I/O backend
vldb2023-52.state_or_ir: User-space task queues (ready, waiting for I/O)
vldb2023-52.output: A schedule of I/O requests and task executions on a fixed number of worker threads.
vldb2023-52.equation: OS-managed threading + Bo + Ha -> DBMS-managed cooperative multitasking
vldb2023-52.principle_composition_rationale: The design is fundamentally Hardware-Aware (Ha), recognizing that modern NVMe arrays require extreme software parallelism to saturate. This awareness reveals that the primary limiter is not storage but CPU overhead, making the design Bottleneck-Oriented (Bo). Together, these principles motivate a shift from an OS-centric concurrency model to an application-managed one that is tailored to the hardware's characteristics and avoids the OS scheduling bottleneck.
vldb2023-52.one_sentence_mechanism: To saturate highly parallel NVMe SSD arrays, LeanStore replaces OS-managed threads and synchronous I/O with a DBMS-managed cooperative multitasking scheduler using lightweight tasks to eliminate context-switching bottlenecks.

key_deltas.summary: Replaced OS-managed threads and synchronous I/O with a DBMS-managed cooperative multitasking scheduler that uses lightweight user-space tasks and non-blocking I/O to avoid OS context switching overhead.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Requires rewriting the system to use non-preemptive multitasking and asynchronous I/O calls. All potentially blocking operations, including latch acquisitions, must be modified to yield to the scheduler.

key_deltas.summary: Integrated background work (e.g., page eviction) into worker threads as system tasks and made the I/O path scalable by replacing a global lock with partitioned locks.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Worker threads now spend CPU cycles on background work, which must be balanced against processing user tasks. The design creates a more symmetric but more tightly integrated system.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The implementation of a cooperative, user-space multitasking scheduler.
Bo.why_it_applies: The entire motivation for replacing OS threads is to eliminate the CPU bottleneck caused by OS context switching overhead when trying to saturate NVMe arrays with thousands of concurrent requests.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: Replacing a global lock with partitioned locks and integrating page eviction into worker threads.
Bo.why_it_applies: These mechanisms directly address two identified performance bottlenecks: the global lock on the I/O path becoming a contention point, and dedicated page provider threads being too slow or hard to tune.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: The use of asynchronous I/O and a scheduler capable of managing thousands of in-flight requests.
Ha.why_it_applies: The design is a direct response to the hardware characteristics of NVMe SSDs, which require a very high I/O depth ('around 1000 concurrent I/O requests... and 3000 to fully saturate the system') to achieve maximum throughput.

Si.name: Simplicity
Si.mechanism_trigger: Integrating background work (page eviction) into the main worker threads instead of using dedicated background threads.
Si.why_it_applies: This creates a symmetric design where all threads have the same roles, which removes the need for specialized background threads and eliminates the difficult tuning parameter of how many such threads to run.

Dc.name: Decentralized Control
Dc.mechanism_trigger: Replacing a single global lock for I/O management with multiple locks partitioned by pageId.
Dc.why_it_applies: This mechanism explicitly decentralizes locking authority from a single point to many, reducing contention among worker threads performing I/O and eviction.

vldb2023-53.paper_title: NV-SQL: Boosting OLTP Performance with Non-Volatile DIMMs
vldb2023-53.goal: To mitigate the durability overhead in relational DBMSs with flash SSDs by introducing NVDIMM as a durable write cache to absorb a large fraction of writes, thereby boosting transaction throughput.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Higher transaction throughput compared to a same-priced vanilla MySQL with larger DRAM.
Reduction in writes to storage by placing redo buffer and DWB in NVDIMM.
Reduction in recovery time by skipping redo for durable write-hot pages in NVDIMM.
Reduction in 99th percentile transaction latency compared to Vanilla MySQL.

vldb2023-53.limiting_design_assumption: The system uses a two-tier memory hierarchy of volatile DRAM and non-volatile SSD. This forces all durability operations (log flushes, checkpoints, dirty page evictions) to go through the slow software storage stack to the SSD, creating a performance bottleneck from 'durability overhead'.
vldb2023-53.state_or_ir: Database pages in the buffer pool, redo log buffer, Double Write Buffer (DWB).
vldb2023-53.output: A decision to migrate a dirty page to NVDIMM or write it to SSD, and a modified logging/recovery protocol that leverages NVDIMM's persistence.
vldb2023-53.equation: DRAM-SSD DBMS with Volatile Buffering + Ha + Wa + Cz -> NV-SQL
vldb2023-53.principle_composition_rationale: The design is fundamentally Hardware-Aware (Ha), introducing NVDIMM to a traditional architecture. This new hardware is used in a Workload-Aware (Wa) fashion to selectively cache the most impactful data (write-hot pages). The persistence of this hardware is then further exploited to Avoid Coordination (Cz) with slow storage during the critical commit protocol, compounding the performance gains.
vldb2023-53.one_sentence_mechanism: NV-SQL introduces a small, fast NVDIMM as a durable write cache, using LSN-based policies to admit only write-hot pages and placing logging structures there to eliminate synchronous I/O to SSD.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a non-hierarchical NVDIMM durable write cache alongside the DRAM buffer and uses workload-aware admission policies based on checkpointing behavior and page LSNs to selectively cache only write-hot pages, absorbing writes that would otherwise go to SSD.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: For the same system cost, using NVDIMM reduces the amount of DRAM available, which can lower the overall buffer hit ratio. It also adds complexity to the buffer manager.

key_deltas.summary: Leverages NVDIMM's persistence to optimize logging and recovery by placing the redo buffer and Double Write Buffer (DWB) in NVDIMM, and introduces a mechanism to ensure page-action consistency for durably cached pages.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Adds CPU overhead for manipulating the in-update flag and issuing `clflush` instructions to ensure persistence, though this is offset by avoiding much slower SSD writes.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: The introduction of NVDIMM as a new tier in the memory hierarchy, chosen for its specific properties of DRAM-like speed, byte-addressability, and persistence.
Ha.why_it_applies: The entire design of the durable write cache is predicated on exploiting the unique performance characteristics of NVDIMM-N, which are different from both DRAM (non-persistent) and SSDs (slow writes, block-based).

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: Placing the redo buffer and Double Write Buffer (DWB) in NVDIMM.
Ha.why_it_applies: This mechanism directly exploits the persistence property of NVDIMM to eliminate expensive I/O protocols (log-force-at-commit, DWB writes) that are necessary when these buffers are in volatile DRAM.

Wa.name: Workload-Aware
Wa.mechanism_trigger: The re-update interval-based and checkpoint-based admission policies that use LSNs and dirty page list position to identify 'write-hot' pages.
Wa.why_it_applies: The system explicitly identifies and adapts to OLTP workload characteristics like temporal locality and write skews. The admission policies are designed to dynamically detect which pages are part of the 'write working set' and selectively cache them.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The system's goal of absorbing a 'large fraction of writes written from DRAM to SSD' to mitigate the 'durability overhead'.
Bo.why_it_applies: The paper's central argument is that writes to SSD are the primary performance bottleneck in write-intensive OLTP workloads. The NVDIMM cache is designed specifically to alleviate this pressure point.

Cz.name: Coordination Avoidance
Cz.mechanism_trigger: Eliminating the 'log-force-at-commit' protocol by placing the redo buffer in NVDIMM.
Cz.why_it_applies: The baseline system must perform a synchronous I/O to the log device before a transaction can commit, coordinating the CPU with the slow storage device. By making the redo buffer durable in NVDIMM, this synchronous coordination is avoided, removing the flush latency from the critical path.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance
Wv.mechanism_trigger: During recovery, skipping the replay of redo logs for NVDIMM-resident pages whose 'in-update flag' is not set.
Wv.why_it_applies: The system can prove that these pages are in an action-consistent state due to the flag mechanism. Replaying the redo logs for them is unnecessary work that would not change their state. This skipped work is guaranteed not to alter the correct, recovered database state.

vldb2023-54.paper_title: Dalton: Learned Partitioning for Distributed Data Streams
vldb2023-54.goal: To design a lightweight, adaptive, and scalable partitioning operator for distributed data streams that can handle skewed data and distribution shifts without a centralized partitioner becoming a bottleneck.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Higher throughput compared to existing approaches.
Higher throughput achieved by the distributed learning protocol compared to simply replicating the partitioner.

vldb2023-54.baseline_name: Cost-aware adaptive partitioners such as DAGreedy, plus stateless or naively replicated partitioners
vldb2023-54.limiting_design_assumption: A cost-aware partitioner can make each hot-key routing decision by greedily evaluating the load-imbalance plus reducer-aggregation cost model for candidate combiners on every tuple, while hashing the long tail; additionally, a single or naively replicated partitioner is sufficient to keep the policy coherent at stream scale.
vldb2023-54.primary_tool_specific_type: Contextual bandit with distributed Q-table reconciliation
vldb2023-54.state_or_ir: Q-tables, one per heavy-hitter key, storing the estimated reward for assigning the key to each possible combiner.
vldb2023-54.output: The selected combiner for each incoming tuple.
vldb2023-54.equation: DAGreedy-style Cost-Aware Heavy-Hitter Partitioning + La + Ad + Rc + Dc -> Dalton
vldb2023-54.principle_composition_rationale: Dalton keeps the cost-aware heavy-hitter/tail split from prior stream-partitioning work, but changes how the expensive hot-key decision is made and scaled. Workload-Aware Optimisation (Wa) identifies the hot keys that deserve special treatment, Learned Approximation (La) turns the cost objective into reward estimates, Adaptive Processing (Ad) updates those estimates online, Reuse of Computation (Rc) lets Q-table values stand in for repeated per-tuple cost-model evaluation, Common-Case Specialization (Cc) keeps tail keys on a cheap hashing path, Decentralized Control (Dc) lets multiple partitioners learn locally, and Coordination Avoidance (Cz) keeps those partitioners off the per-tuple coordination path while the QtableReducer periodically reconciles their policies.
vldb2023-54.one_sentence_mechanism: Dalton replaces repeated per-tuple greedy cost-model evaluation for heavy-hitter routing with reusable contextual-bandit Q-table rewards, while multiple local partitioners asynchronously reconcile their policies through the QtableReducer.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces repeated per-tuple greedy cost-model evaluation for heavy-hitter keys with a stateful learned reward table.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Introduces memory overhead for Q-tables (O(n^2) for n heavy hitters and n combiners), exploration/convergence behavior, and bandit-update overhead, while avoiding repeated full candidate cost-model evaluation.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a cooperative learning protocol in which multiple partitioners learn locally and asynchronously reconcile their policies through the QtableReducer.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Adds synchronization and communication overhead, stale-policy windows between synchronization points, and a centralized reducer that must not be overloaded.

La.name: Learned Approximation
La.mechanism_trigger: The use of a contextual bandit to learn a partitioning policy.
La.why_it_applies: A learned model (the Q-table) approximates the routing value of assigning each heavy-hitter key to each combiner, replacing direct per-tuple greedy cost evaluation with learned reward estimates.

Ad.name: Adaptive Processing
Ad.mechanism_trigger: The online Q-table updates and ε-greedy policy adaptation as the data distribution and load state change.
Ad.why_it_applies: Dalton continuously adapts its routing policy from observed reward feedback. This is an important fit, though adaptation alone does not distinguish Dalton from DAGreedy, which is also adaptive.

Ad.name: Adaptive Processing
Ad.mechanism_trigger: The protocol for dynamically adjusting the synchronization interval (DSYNC) based on the observed latency from the QTableReducer.
Ad.why_it_applies: The system has a secondary control loop that adapts the communication frequency at runtime to balance convergence speed and synchronization overhead.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The Q-table stores reward estimates from prior routing decisions and reuses them for future hot-key assignments.
Rc.why_it_applies: The reused computation is past routing experience. Dalton avoids recomputing the full cost-model score for every candidate combiner on every tuple by consulting learned Q-table values.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The periodic aggregation of local Q-tables into a global Q-table, which is then broadcast back to all partitioners.
Rc.why_it_applies: This is a second form of reuse: partitioners reuse other partitioners' learned experience through the aggregated global policy.

Cc.name: Common-Case Specialization
Cc.mechanism_trigger: The hybrid scheme that applies the learning-based partitioner only to heavy-hitter keys and uses simple hashing for the long tail of infrequent keys.
Cc.why_it_applies: The system separates the workload into a 'common case' (frequent keys causing skew) and a 'rare case' (infrequent keys) and applies a specialized, more expensive mechanism to the former and a cheap, fast-path mechanism to the latter.

Wa.name: Workload-Aware Optimisation
Wa.mechanism_trigger: Dalton identifies heavy-hitter keys and applies learned routing to those skew-driving keys while using hashing for the long tail.
Wa.why_it_applies: The partitioner is organized around workload properties: hot keys receive an adaptive learned policy because they dominate load imbalance and reducer aggregation cost, while infrequent keys stay on a cheaper path.

Dc.name: Decentralized Control
Dc.mechanism_trigger: Deploying multiple parallel partitioner instances, each making local decisions, to overcome the bottleneck of a single partitioner.
Dc.why_it_applies: The system explicitly moves from a centralized partitioner model to one with multiple, distributed partitioner agents to improve scalability. The protocol coordinates these decentralized agents.

Cz.name: Coordination Avoidance
Cz.mechanism_trigger: Multiple partitioners make local routing decisions and synchronize their Q-tables periodically through the QtableReducer instead of coordinating on every tuple.
Cz.why_it_applies: Dalton avoids per-decision coordination among partitioners while still reconciling interacting local policies asynchronously. The synchronization interval is tuned so partitioners can act independently without overloading the QtableReducer.

vldb2023-55.paper_title: Efficient framework for operating on data sketches
vldb2023-55.goal: To create a framework for estimating the weighted cardinality of a set constructed from any sequence of set-theory operations on massive data streams, while significantly improving the computational efficiency of sketch creation.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Reduction in the average number of comparisons per stream element to create a sketch, where n is the number of stream elements.
Time taken by the baseline (ExpSketch) versus the proposed system (FastExpSketch) to generate 30 sketches for a Twitter dataset.
The relative standard error of the cardinality estimate for a sketch of size m.

vldb2023-55.limiting_design_assumption: The baseline assumes that for each of the n stream elements, it is necessary to generate m random values and perform m point-wise comparisons against the m slots in the sketch, resulting in O(m*n) complexity.
vldb2023-55.state_or_ir: A sketch M, which is an array of m floating-point values representing minimums of exponential random variables.
vldb2023-55.equation: O(m*n) Sketch Updates + Wv + Gr + Ep -> FastExpSketch
vldb2023-55.principle_composition_rationale: The design uses Equivalence-based (Ep) transformations as a foundational tool for both performance and functionality. For performance, a distributional equivalence enables Work Avoidance (Wv) in the sketch update algorithm. For functionality, an algebraic equivalence (FDNF) enables a Generalized Design (Gr) that extends estimation from a few specific set operations to an entire algebra.
vldb2023-55.one_sentence_mechanism: Reduces sketch update costs from O(m) to O(log n) per element by generating order statistics and stopping early, and generalizes query power to arbitrary set expressions by decomposing them into a disjoint normal form.

key_deltas.summary: Reduces sketch update time by generating order statistics of random values sequentially and stopping early, avoiding the generation of values guaranteed to be too large to affect the sketch.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The algorithm is more complex, requiring tracking of the sketch's maximum value and logic for partial permutation generation.

key_deltas.summary: Generalizes sketch-based estimation from a few specific set operations to any arbitrary sequence of set-theory operations by decomposing the expression into a disjoint normal form.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The conversion of a set expression to FDNF or DDNF can be computationally expensive, potentially exponential in the number of sets.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance: skip work that cannot alter externally observable results
Wv.mechanism_trigger: The early-exit condition in the loop of Algorithm 2, which breaks when the k-th order statistic S exceeds the current maximum value MAX in the sketch.
Wv.why_it_applies: The algorithm avoids generating the remaining m-k order statistics because they are guaranteed to be larger than S and thus larger than MAX. Since the sketch update operation is a minimum, these larger values cannot change the sketch's state. The skipped work is proven to be irrelevant to the final result.

Ep.name: Equivalence-based
Ep.mechanism_trigger: The use of Theorem 4.1, which establishes a distributional equality between a set of i.i.d. exponential variables and their order statistics.
Ep.why_it_applies: The entire optimization is predicated on the fact that the new procedure using order statistics produces an identical sketch to the baseline. This equivalence allows the system to switch to a more efficient but mathematically equivalent formulation.

Ep.name: Equivalence-based
Ep.mechanism_trigger: The transformation of a set-theory expression into a full disjunctive normal form (FDNF).
Ep.why_it_applies: This step uses standard, equivalence-preserving rules from boolean algebra (e.g., de Morgan's laws, distributivity) to rewrite the input expression into a canonical form that is amenable to estimation. The correctness of the final estimate depends on this transformation being equivalent.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The design of the FastExpSketch algorithm to specifically reduce the number of comparisons.
Bo.why_it_applies: The paper explicitly identifies the O(m*n) cost of generating values and comparisons as the performance bottleneck that can make sketch creation 'take an unacceptable amount of time'. The new algorithm is designed specifically to alleviate this bottleneck.

Gr.name: Generalized Design
Gr.mechanism_trigger: The development of a scheme to handle any set-theory expression, not just specific ones like union or intersection.
Gr.why_it_applies: The paper generalizes the solution from [18] which handled a few specific operations to a framework that can estimate the result of 'any sequence of set-theory operations'. The FDNF/DDNF mechanism is the key to this generalization.

Co.name: Composability
Co.mechanism_trigger: The final estimation step, which sums the estimates of the disjoint components derived from the FDNF.
Co.why_it_applies: The framework decomposes a complex expression into disjoint parts, estimates each part independently, and then composes the final result by summing these estimates. This relies on the disjointness of the components to allow for simple composition (addition).

vldb2023-56.paper_title: Optimistic Data Parallelism for FPGA-Accelerated Sketching
vldb2023-56.goal: To propose a novel optimistic architecture for FPGA-accelerated sketching with an improved trade-off between throughput and resource consumption, specifically by reducing the consumption of scarce on-chip state memory.

concrete_numbers.number_or_delta: up to a factor of d
concrete_numbers.meaning: Reduction in state memory consumption, where d is the number of parallel inputs.
Higher throughput compared to CPU and GPU implementations.

concrete_numbers.number_or_delta: up to an order of magnitude
concrete_numbers.meaning: Improvement in accuracy due to larger sketch sizes enabled by resource savings.
Achieved sketching throughput on a Xilinx U250 FPGA.

vldb2023-56.limiting_design_assumption: To achieve high, guaranteed data-parallel throughput without stalls, the entire sketching hardware and state memory must be replicated for each parallel input stream to completely avoid concurrent access conflicts.
vldb2023-56.primary_tool_specific_type: Hardware architecture for parallel data processing
vldb2023-56.state_or_ir: A single sketch matrix, logically partitioned into independent, concurrently accessible memory banks.
vldb2023-56.output: An updated sketch matrix reflecting aggregated data stream updates.
vldb2023-56.equation: Pessimistic Replication + Op + Ha + Wv -> Optimistic Banked Sketching
vldb2023-56.principle_composition_rationale: This composition trades guaranteed performance for resource efficiency. Optimistic Design (Op) enables sharing of scarce hardware resources (Ha) by abandoning the baseline's worst-case replication. This shift introduces a new performance risk under skewed workloads (contention), which is mitigated by using Work Avoidance (Wv) to skip the redundant memory updates that would cause contention, thereby making the optimistic trade-off viable in practice.
vldb2023-56.one_sentence_mechanism: Replaces pessimistic replication of sketching hardware with a single shared, banked memory architecture, and mitigates the resulting contention under skew by using in-pipeline mergers to coalesce updates before they access memory.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces full replication of sketch state with a single, shared sketch partitioned into independent memory banks, allowing all parallel inputs to access the same state. This trades guaranteed stall-free processing for significantly lower memory resource consumption.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Introduces the possibility of processing stalls if skewed input data causes persistent access conflicts for the same memory bank, which can reduce the effective throughput.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces logic within the dispatcher to merge multiple updates targeting the same sketch entry before they are sent to the memory banks. This reduces the rate of requests to banks under skewed workloads, mitigating the stalls introduced by the optimistic architecture.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Adds significant logic (LUTs, flip-flops) to the dispatcher, which can become a new resource or timing bottleneck, especially for high degrees of parallelism.

Op.name: Optimistic Design
Op.mechanism_trigger: Using a single, shared, banked sketch memory for all parallel inputs instead of fully replicating it.
Op.why_it_applies: The architecture is explicitly named 'optimistic' and proceeds assuming access conflicts are rare or manageable, rather than provisioning for the worst case (pessimistic replication). It accepts stalls as the recovery mechanism for contention.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: Partitioning the sketch state into independent BRAM/URAM banks.
Ha.why_it_applies: The design is explicitly motivated by the scarcity of a specific hardware resource (on-chip FPGA memory) and uses a hardware-specific layout (banking) to improve resource efficiency.

Mo.name: Modularity
Mo.mechanism_trigger: The partitioning of the sketch state into independent banks, each with its own processing pipeline stages.
Mo.why_it_applies: The design decomposes the monolithic state memory into independent, modular units (banks) that can operate in parallel, simplifying the hardware design for parallel access.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance: skip work that cannot alter externally observable results
Wv.mechanism_trigger: Merging multiple increments for the same offset into a single increment before the memory write stage.
Wv.why_it_applies: The associativity and commutativity of the reduce function guarantee that merging increments in-pipeline produces the exact same final sketch state as applying them sequentially. The avoided memory read-modify-write cycles are therefore result-irrelevant work.

Lo.name: Locality of Reference
Lo.mechanism_trigger: The horizontal merger's look-ahead buffer and the vertical merger's check of queue heads for matching offsets.
Lo.why_it_applies: The paper explicitly states that the merging mechanism is designed to exploit temporal locality in the input stream to mitigate the effects of data skew.

Wa.name: Workload-Aware
Wa.mechanism_trigger: The inclusion of the merging logic itself, which is specifically designed to handle skewed data distributions and heavy hitters.
Wa.why_it_applies: The design is explicitly aware that skewed workloads are a problem and incorporates a static mechanism (merging) to handle that specific workload characteristic, making the system robust to skew.

vldb2023-57.paper_title: Out-of-Order Sliding-Window Aggregation with Efficient Bulk Evictions and Insertions
vldb2023-57.goal: To design a sliding-window aggregation algorithm that efficiently handles bulk evictions and bulk insertions for out-of-order and bursty data streams.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Amortized time complexity for bulk eviction of m items.
Amortized time complexity for bulk insertion of m items with out-of-order distance d.
Time complexity of the baseline (FiBA) emulating bulk operations, which is improved upon.

vldb2023-57.limiting_design_assumption: Stream data items are inserted or evicted one at a time. Bulk operations must be emulated by looping over single-item operations, which is inefficient for bursts.
vldb2023-57.state_or_ir: An augmented finger B-tree (the FiBA data structure) that stores timestamped values and partial aggregates.
vldb2023-57.output: An updated B-tree reflecting the bulk insertion or eviction, allowing for constant-time queries of the new aggregate.
vldb2023-57.equation: FiBA (Item-at-a-time) + Wv (Work Avoidance) + Rc (Reuse of Computation) -> Bulk FiBA
vldb2023-57.principle_composition_rationale: These principles transform an item-at-a-time algorithm into a burst-aware one. Wv provides the insight for eviction: most per-item work is unnecessary if you operate on the batch boundary. Rc provides the insight for insertion: per-item work can be shared if you process the batch collectively. Together, they replace a loop of `m` operations with a single, more efficient batch operation.
vldb2023-57.one_sentence_mechanism: Replaces item-at-a-time updates on an augmented B-tree with native bulk operations that avoid redundant work by operating on the eviction boundary (for deletes) and sharing update paths via level-wise processing (for inserts).

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a sub-linear time bulk eviction algorithm that replaces item-by-item deletion with a single, boundary-based tree modification.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Increased algorithmic complexity and the need for a deferred free list to avoid O(m) memory deallocation costs, which would negate the O(log m) time complexity.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a native bulk insertion algorithm that processes a batch of insertions in a single, level-by-level pass up the tree, sharing work for items that affect common subpaths.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Increased algorithmic complexity, requiring intermediate data structures ('treelets') and a level-by-level processing loop to manage the batched updates.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance: skip work that cannot alter externally observable results
Wv.mechanism_trigger: The bulk eviction algorithm.
Wv.why_it_applies: The algorithm explicitly avoids processing each of the `m` items to be evicted. Instead, it finds the single boundary and performs a tree cut. The work of traversing the tree for each of the other `m-1` items is skipped entirely, as it cannot change the final state of the tree, which is determined solely by the eviction timestamp `t`.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The single pass up and down the tree during bulk eviction.
Rc.why_it_applies: Instead of `m` separate traversals and repair passes, the algorithm performs a single shared search to find the boundary and single shared passes to repair tree invariants and aggregates. The computation of the path and the repair logic is effectively shared across all `m` evicted items.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The level-by-level interleave-and-split loop for bulk insertion.
Rc.why_it_applies: The algorithm is explicitly designed to share work. By processing insertions level-by-level and grouping 'treelets' by their target node, the work of traversing the upper parts of the tree, splitting nodes, and recomputing aggregates is performed only once for all insertions that converge on the same path. This reuses the computation of path traversal and node modification.

vldb2023-58.paper_title: High-Performance Row Pattern Recognition Using Joins
vldb2023-58.goal: To accelerate MATCH_RECOGNIZE queries for row pattern recognition in relational systems under historical analysis settings.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Median speedup on Trino (v373 with ORC files on Hive).
Median speedup on SQL Server (2019) using column store.
Median speedup on SQL Server (2019) using row store.

vldb2023-58.limiting_design_assumption: The NFA-based approach must consume and process input rows sequentially, one-by-one. This streaming-oriented design overlooks optimization opportunities available in a historical analysis (batch) context where all data is available, such as flexible execution order and pre-filtering.
vldb2023-58.output: A rewritten logical query plan that uses joins to filter the input to the NFA operator.
vldb2023-58.equation: Sequential NFA Matching + Ep + Cm + Wv -> JoinNFA
vldb2023-58.principle_composition_rationale: JoinNFA is best framed primarily around Equivalence-based Planning (Ep) and Cost-based Planning (Cm), with Work Avoidance (Wv) as a supporting principle. Ep captures the result-preserving rewrite: the join-based prefilter can be inserted because the paper proves it preserves MATCH_RECOGNIZE results. Cm captures the optimizer choice: the rewrite is applied only when the estimated filtering benefit is expected to outweigh the added join cost. Wv names the performance effect of that rewrite: fewer irrelevant rows reach the expensive NFA operator.
vldb2023-58.one_sentence_mechanism: Rewrites sequential NFA-based row-pattern matching into a result-preserving join prefilter plus NFA plan, using a cost model to decide when the added joins are worthwhile and thereby reduce unnecessary NFA work.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a join-based prefiltering strategy that rewrites a MATCH_RECOGNIZE query to first identify temporal 'envelopes' that could contain a match, and then filters the input table to only these rows before executing the NFA.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The prefiltering joins add overhead. If the joins are not selective, the rewritten plan can be slower. This also increases query optimization time due to the need to search for a good symbol set.

key_deltas.summary: Develops a cost-based optimization framework to select the best prefiltering plan and introduces a bucketization technique to make prefiltering efficient even without indexes.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The cost models add complexity and optimization overhead. Bucketization is an approximation that can be less selective than precise range joins, but this is traded for significantly faster hash join execution.

Ep.name: Equivalence-based Planning
Ep.mechanism_trigger: The join-based prefilter is introduced as a logical rewrite of the MATCH_RECOGNIZE plan, with propositions proving that the filtered input preserves the original query result.
Ep.why_it_applies: Ep captures the central principle in JoinNFA: replace the sequential NFA-only plan with a result-preserving relational rewrite. The prefilter is valid because running the NFA on the filtered relation returns the same matches as running it on the original relation.

Ep.name: Equivalence-based
Ep.mechanism_trigger: The bucketization technique, which transforms range join conditions into equality join conditions on computed bucket IDs.
Ep.why_it_applies: Bucketization is a specific plan rewrite that transforms a query with expensive band joins into a semantically valid (though potentially less selective) plan with efficient hash joins. This transformation between plan structures is a form of equivalence-based rewriting.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance
Wv.mechanism_trigger: The result-preserving join-based prefilter narrows the input before it reaches the expensive NFA operator.
Wv.why_it_applies: Wv is useful as a secondary principle here: it names the performance effect enabled by the equivalence-preserving rewrite, not the correctness basis of the rewrite. After Ep establishes that filtering preserves the result, JoinNFA can avoid NFA work on rows that cannot participate in any match.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The design of the prefilter to reduce the input cardinality to the NFA operator.
Bo.why_it_applies: The paper identifies the high number of partial matching states in the NFA as a key performance bottleneck, stating 'most of the computation is wasted'. The entire prefiltering mechanism is designed to alleviate this specific bottleneck by reducing the number of rows the NFA must process.

Cm.name: Cost-based Planning
Cm.mechanism_trigger: The optimizer uses cost models for the prefilter and NFA operator to choose the symbol set for the rewrite, or to skip the rewrite when it is not worthwhile.
Cm.why_it_applies: Cm captures the planning principle for choosing among rewrite options. It determines when the result-preserving rewrite is expected to pay off by comparing filtering benefit against join and optimization overhead.

vldb2023-59.paper_title: HMAB: Self-Driving Hierarchy of Bandits for Integrated Physical Database Design Tuning
vldb2023-59.goal: To recommend a sequence of physical design structure (PDS) configurations that minimizes the total workload time for a given workload sequence, without prior knowledge of the workload.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Performance gain (speed-up) over a state-of-the-art commercial physical design tool under dynamic workloads.
Reduction in PDS creation time compared to methods that rely solely on execution statistics (i.e., without hypothetical checks).
Drop in recommendation running time compared to a monolithic bandit (DBABandit) on the TPC-DS benchmark, demonstrating the scalability of the hierarchical architecture.

vldb2023-59.baseline_name: PDTool-style optimizer-based physical design tuning, plus non-hierarchical bandit tuning
vldb2023-59.limiting_design_assumption: The database optimizer's what-if estimates are accurate enough to rank physical design structures (PDSs), and the integrated search over indexes and materialized views can be handled without explicitly exploiting the compositional, hierarchical structure of physical design.
vldb2023-59.state_or_ir: A hierarchical arm representation: L1 bandits maintain scoped PDS candidates and reward estimates, and the L2 bandit selects budget-constrained configurations from the composed candidate set. Context vectors encode PDS features such as column composition, PDS type, size, and existence.
vldb2023-59.output: A recommended PDS configuration (a 'super arm') for the next workload round.
vldb2023-59.equation: PDTool-style PDS Tuning + La + Mo + Ad + Wv -> HMAB
vldb2023-59.principle_composition_rationale: The composition addresses the core challenge of integrated PDS tuning: finding high-quality configurations in a vast, structured search space without relying only on optimizer cost estimates. La learns rewards of PDS arms from actual execution feedback. Mo is central because the hierarchy exploits relationships among physical design structures to make search tractable. Ad refines recommendations online as workload observations accumulate. Wv makes exploration cheaper by using what-if analysis to prune candidate structures before paying materialization cost.
vldb2023-59.one_sentence_mechanism: HMAB augments PDTool-style physical design tuning with a modular hierarchy of bandits that learns rewards of indexes and materialized views from execution feedback, while using what-if checks to prune unnecessary design-space exploration before materialization.

key_deltas.summary: Augments optimizer-based physical design with a bandit framework that learns rewards of PDSs from direct execution feedback.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Incurs PDS creation costs during the exploration phase, though the paper argues the total exploration cost (recommendation + creation) is often lower than the baseline's.

key_deltas.summary: Uses a modular hierarchy of bandits to exploit the compositional structure of PDSs and reduce the search space, with lightweight what-if checks to prune unnecessary exploration.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The quality of the final recommendation depends on the quality of candidates proposed by L1 bandits and on whether the hierarchy captures useful relationships among PDSs. The hypothetical checks add to recommendation time.

La.name: Learned Approximation
La.mechanism_trigger: The C²UCB bandit models the expected reward of a PDS (arm) as a linear function of its context vector, learned from past observations of actual performance.
La.why_it_applies: This learned model (𝜽'𝒙) estimates the reward of physical design structures from execution feedback. The approximation is over PDS rewards/benefits, not over query results and not merely over optimizer-estimated costs.
La.pointer: [p. 218] with 𝜽'𝒙𝑡(𝑖) + 𝜀𝑡(𝑖) for unknown zero-mean... random variable 𝜀𝑡..."]

Ad.name: Adaptive Processing
Ad.mechanism_trigger: The system operates online, observing a sequence of workloads and updating its PDS configuration in each round based on historical performance.
Ad.why_it_applies: HMAB continuously refines recommendations as workload observations and execution feedback accumulate. This captures the online self-driving aspect, while the hierarchy captures the structured-search aspect.

Wa.name: Workload-Aware
Wa.mechanism_trigger: The set of potential PDSs (arms) considered by the bandits is generated dynamically based on the queries observed in the recent workload.
Wa.why_it_applies: The system focuses its search on PDSs that are relevant to the current workload, pruning the vast space of all possible PDSs.

Mo.name: Modularity
Mo.mechanism_trigger: The system is decomposed into a two-layer hierarchy. The L1 bandits are modules, each responsible for a specific scope (e.g., indices for a single table, or all views).
Mo.why_it_applies: This decomposition exploits the compositional nature of physical design structures and breaks the complex integrated tuning problem into smaller, manageable subproblems. The paper highlights that L1 bandits can be added or removed without disrupting the rest of the system.

Co.name: Composability
Co.mechanism_trigger: The L2 bandit takes the candidate PDSs recommended by the modular L1 bandits as its input action space.
Co.why_it_applies: The L2 bandit composes the outputs of the L1 modules to form a single, globally budget-constrained PDS configuration, achieving an integrated search.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance
Wv.mechanism_trigger: The hypothetical check mechanism uses the optimizer's what-if API to identify which of the bandit's selected PDSs would actually be used in a query plan before materialization.
Wv.why_it_applies: The avoided work is unnecessary exploration of the design space: expensive materialization and evaluation of candidate structures that the optimizer would not use. This is not about avoiding query work during normal execution; it is about making recommendation-time exploration cheaper while preserving recommendation quality.

vldb2023-60.goal: To design an elastic, performant, and cost-effective cloud storage solution using serverless functions that can fulfill the heterogeneous and dynamic storage requirements of a wide variety of applications.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Tenant-side cost reduction compared to AWS ElastiCache.
Tenant-side cost reduction compared to InfiniCache.
Cost overhead for recovery and warmup activities over an ideal pay-per-access scheme.
Average time to recover a 3,008 MB Lambda function instance using 20 parallel recovery functions.

vldb2023-60.limiting_design_assumption: A static, fixed-size pool of serverless functions is sufficient for caching, and durability should be handled via primary/backup replication within the ephemeral function layer.
vldb2023-60.primary_tool_specific_type: Workload-aware memory management for serverless functions
vldb2023-60.state_or_ir: A metadata table mapping object chunks to function instances, where functions are organized into time-based 'GC-buckets' representing a sliding window.
vldb2023-60.output: A durable, elastic, and low-cost storage service with GET/PUT APIs.
vldb2023-60.equation: Static Replicated FaaS Cache + Wa + Ec + Ft -> Elastic Recoverable FaaS Storage
vldb2023-60.principle_composition_rationale: This composition transforms a simple FaaS-based cache into a durable storage system. Workload-Awareness (Wa) provides the intelligence to distinguish hot from cold data, which enables Elasticity (Ec) to efficiently manage the pool of ephemeral functions by only keeping resources for hot data active. Fault Tolerance (Ft) through fast parallel recovery makes this highly dynamic and ephemeral memory layer robust enough to serve as the primary tier of a durable storage system, addressing the inherent unreliability of FaaS functions.
vldb2023-60.one_sentence_mechanism: InfiniStore builds an elastic and durable storage system by using a sliding-window, garbage-collection-inspired policy to manage serverless functions as a workload-aware memory tier, combined with a fast, parallel recovery scheme from a persistent object store.

key_deltas.summary: Replaced InfiniCache's static, randomly-mapped function pool with a dynamic, sliding-window mechanism that segregates hot/cold data based on temporal locality, achieving fine-grained elasticity and cost savings.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Adds management complexity to the client daemon. Accessing cold data in a reclaimed function incurs a miss, requiring a slower fetch from the backing object store.

key_deltas.summary: Replaced InfiniCache's in-memory primary/backup replication with a single-copy memory layer backed by a persistent object store, using a fast, parallel recovery scheme to handle frequent function reclamations.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Recovery from reclamation is not instantaneous and introduces overhead for maintaining and persisting insertion logs to the object store.

Wa.name: Workload-Aware
Wa.mechanism_trigger: The sliding-window and GC-bucket mechanism that segregates data based on access time and reuse intervals.
Wa.why_it_applies: The system's data placement and function reclamation strategy is explicitly designed around the observed temporal access patterns (WSS, reuse intervals) of workloads to separate hot and cold data.

Ec.name: Elasticity
Ec.mechanism_trigger: The automatic invocation of new functions when storage demand surges and the cessation of heartbeats for functions holding cold data, allowing them to be reclaimed.
Ec.why_it_applies: The system's primary goal is to automatically and rapidly grow and shrink its storage capacity (the number of active functions) in response to changing application demands (WSS).

Cc.name: Common-Case Specialization
Cc.mechanism_trigger: The use of 'active' GC-buckets with a frequent warmup policy versus 'degraded' buckets with a less frequent policy.
Cc.why_it_applies: The system optimizes for the common case of accessing hot data by keeping it in actively managed functions, while accesses to cold data are treated as a less common case that can tolerate a higher-latency fetch from the backing store.

Ft.name: Fault Tolerance
Ft.mechanism_trigger: The parallel recovery scheme using a group of recovery functions and an insertion log to restore data after a storage function is reclaimed.
Ft.why_it_applies: The entire mechanism is designed to make the system robust against the frequent 'faults' of function instance reclamation, which is an inherent property of the FaaS environment.

Fp.name: Function Placement
Fp.mechanism_trigger: The two-layer architecture with the ServerlessMemory Store (SMS) for fast access and the Cloud Object Store (COS) for persistence.
Fp.why_it_applies: The design makes a deliberate choice to disaggregate the system, placing the function of fast, elastic data access in the SMS layer and the function of durable, persistent storage in the separate COS layer.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The use of tens of recovery functions to recover lost data in parallel, rather than a single function recovering all data serially.
Bo.why_it_applies: The recovery time after a function reclamation is a critical performance bottleneck. The design directly targets this bottleneck by parallelizing the recovery process to minimize downtime.

vldb2023-61.paper_title: Exploiting Cloud Object Storage for High-Performance Analytics
vldb2023-61.problem_domain: Analytical query processing on cloud object stores
vldb2023-61.goal: To perform efficient analytics directly on cloud object stores, achieving performance similar to systems that cache data on local SSDs, while improving resource elasticity.

concrete_numbers.meaning: AnyBlob achieves the same maximum throughput as the best AWS SDK competitor (S3Crt) using only 70% of the CPU resources.
End-to-end bandwidth achieved by Umbra with AnyBlob on TPC-H Query 1, close to the 100 Gbit/s instance limit.
Umbra with AnyBlob achieves up to 1.2x better geometric mean performance on TPC-H compared to using the asynchronous AWS SDK.

vldb2023-61.baseline_name: Cloud-native data warehouses that rely on local caching
vldb2023-61.limiting_design_assumption: High-performance analytics on cloud object stores requires a local caching layer (e.g., on NVMe SSDs) to hide the high latency and achieve high bandwidth, as direct access is too slow or CPU-intensive.
vldb2023-61.state_or_ir: A queue of download requests for immutable columnar data blocks.
vldb2023-61.output: A stream of data blocks delivered to the query engine's buffer manager.
vldb2023-61.equation: Caching-reliant Cloud DW + Ha + Ad + Bo -> Umbra on Object Storage
vldb2023-61.principle_composition_rationale: The design replaces a coarse-grained caching layer with a fine-grained, adaptive pipeline. It first uses a Hardware-Aware (Ha) I/O layer to solve the CPU Bottleneck (Bo) of high-concurrency networking. This efficient I/O is then managed by an Adaptive (Ad) scheduler that dynamically balances the I/O and compute stages of the query pipeline, preventing either from becoming the new bottleneck (Bo) and ensuring high end-to-end performance without a cache.
vldb2023-61.one_sentence_mechanism: Achieves high-performance analytics directly on cloud object storage by using a low-overhead `io_uring`-based download manager to saturate network bandwidth and an adaptive scheduler to dynamically balance worker threads between data retrieval and query processing.

key_deltas.summary: Introduced AnyBlob, a download manager using the io_uring kernel interface to handle hundreds of concurrent object store requests per thread, saturating high-bandwidth networks with significantly lower CPU overhead than traditional thread-per-request SDKs.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Increased implementation complexity due to using a low-level, modern kernel interface (io_uring) instead of standard, more portable networking libraries. This ties the implementation to recent Linux kernels.

key_deltas.summary: Integrated data retrieval and query processing via an adaptive scheduler that dynamically assigns worker threads from a common pool to either download data, prepare download requests, or execute query logic, balancing the pipeline to maximize end-to-end throughput.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Tighter coupling between the query execution engine and the networking layer. The scheduler adds complexity and relies on runtime statistics and heuristics to make decisions, which may not be optimal in all cases.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: The use of the `io_uring` Linux kernel interface.
Ha.why_it_applies: The design of AnyBlob is centered on `io_uring`, a modern kernel feature that provides low-overhead, asynchronous access to network devices by using shared memory ring buffers, thus avoiding costly system calls and context switches. This is a direct exploitation of a specific OS/hardware interface for performance.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The explicit design goal of minimizing the CPU footprint of network retrieval.
Bo.why_it_applies: The paper identifies the high CPU overhead of networking with traditional libraries as a key bottleneck that consumes resources needed for query processing. AnyBlob is designed specifically to alleviate this bottleneck, enabling the system to saturate the network while preserving CPU cycles for analytics.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The scheduler's goal of balancing retrieval and processing performance.
Bo.why_it_applies: The scheduler's purpose is to prevent either the retrieval stage or the processing stage from becoming a bottleneck that stalls the other. It explicitly balances the two pipeline stages to maximize overall throughput.

Cz.name: Coordination Avoidance
Cz.mechanism_trigger: The use of `io_uring`'s lock-free ring buffers.
Cz.why_it_applies: `io_uring` uses lock-free submission and completion queues shared between user space and the kernel. This avoids the coordination overhead (e.g., kernel locks) associated with traditional, blocking system calls, especially under high concurrency, which is critical for this workload.

Ad.name: Adaptive Processing
Ad.mechanism_trigger: The object scheduler dynamically reassigning worker threads to different jobs (retrieval, preparation, processing) based on runtime performance statistics.
Ad.why_it_applies: The system continuously monitors retrieval and processing speeds and adapts its resource allocation in response. If processing is slower, it shifts threads from retrieval to processing, and vice-versa. This is a classic adaptive control loop.

Wa.name: Workload-Aware
Wa.mechanism_trigger: The scheduler's behavior changing based on whether a query is retrieval-heavy or computation-heavy.
Wa.why_it_applies: The scheduler's adaptation is driven by the specific characteristics of the current query. For a scan-heavy query (e.g., TPC-H Q1), it dedicates more resources to retrieval to saturate the network. For a join-heavy query (e.g., Q9), it dedicates more resources to computation, as networking is not the bottleneck.

vldb2023-62.paper_title: Pando: Enhanced Data Skipping with Logical Data Partitioning
vldb2023-62.goal: To improve query performance in cloud-based database systems by reducing the amount of data accessed and processed, specifically by enhancing data block skipping.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Reduction in the number of blocks scanned compared to state-of-the-art techniques.
Speedup in end-to-end query execution time compared to state-of-the-art techniques.

vldb2023-62.limiting_design_assumption: A single hierarchical tree per table must serve as both the index for data skipping and the physical partitioning function. This tight coupling limits the ability to effectively use correlated predicates for data skipping.
vldb2023-62.state_or_ir: A set of k logical partitioning trees per table and a mapping of tuples to physical blocks.
vldb2023-62.output: A blocked data layout and a set of k logical partitioning trees per table that index the blocks.
vldb2023-62.equation: Single Physical Partitioning Tree (MTO) + Policy/Mechanism (Pm) + Generalized Design (Gr) + Workload-Aware (Wa) -> Pando
vldb2023-62.principle_composition_rationale: Pando's design works by generalizing (Gr) the baseline's single physical partitioning tree. It achieves this by separating the indexing policy from the storage mechanism (Pm), which allows for multiple logical views of the data. This more expressive structure is then tailored to a specific set of queries through a workload-aware (Wa) optimization process.
vldb2023-62.one_sentence_mechanism: Pando decouples physical data layout from indexing by co-optimizing multiple logical partitioning trees with a single physical block assignment to better exploit predicate correlations and reduce I/O.

key_deltas.summary: Decouples logical partitioning from physical layout by introducing multiple logical index trees per table, allowing the system to capture and exploit correlated predicates that a single physical partitioning tree cannot.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Creates an 'extremely large search space' for the optimizer and requires a more complex query execution strategy that intersects block lists from multiple trees.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a two-phase heuristic algorithm to co-optimize the multiple logical trees and the physical block layout, first building trees top-down and then assigning tuples to blocks bottom-up.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Increased offline optimization time compared to the single-phase baseline.

Pm.name: Policy/Mechanism
Pm.mechanism_trigger: The separation of k logical partitioning trees (policy) from the physical block layout (mechanism).
Pm.why_it_applies: This principle perfectly describes the core design move of Pando: 'Decoupling the physical data layout and logical partitioning trees' allows multiple, non-orthogonal indexing policies to be supported by a single, co-optimized physical storage mechanism.

Gr.name: Generalized Design
Gr.mechanism_trigger: Allowing k logical partitioning trees, where the baseline MTO is the special case of k=1.
Gr.why_it_applies: Pando's design is a direct generalization of the baseline. The paper explicitly states 'Pando with k = 1 is the same as MTO', making it a clear example of generalizing a prior design point.

Al.name: Abstraction Lifting
Al.mechanism_trigger: The logical partitioning trees represent logical data subspaces defined by predicates, rather than just physical data chunks.
Al.why_it_applies: The system reasons about logical data spaces ('the left child of the node corresponds to the logical data subspace') and then maps this higher-level abstraction to a physical block layout, which is a form of abstraction lifting.

Wa.name: Workload-Aware
Wa.mechanism_trigger: The entire layout optimization process takes a 'representative workload W' as input.
Wa.why_it_applies: The choice of predicates for the trees and the clustering of tuples into blocks are both driven entirely by the goal of minimizing I/O for the given workload. Predicates are extracted from workload queries.

Cm.name: Cost-based
Cm.mechanism_trigger: The greedy algorithms in both the top-down and bottom-up phases make decisions by selecting the option with the lowest estimated I/O cost.
Cm.why_it_applies: The top-down phase picks the cut with the 'maximum I/O benefit'. The bottom-up phase merges the pair of blocks with the 'lowest cost'. This demonstrates a cost-based approach.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The entire system is designed to minimize block scans from storage.
Bo.why_it_applies: The paper identifies I/O as one of the 'dominant costs for query processing' and the entire mechanism of data skipping is designed to relieve this specific bottleneck.

vldb2023-63.paper_title: Parallelism-Optimizing Data Placement for Faster Data-Parallel Computations
vldb2023-63.problem_domain: Data placement for data-parallel query systems
vldb2023-63.goal: To minimize the tail latency of data-parallel queries by optimizing data placement to maximize query parallelism.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Reduction in p99 latency compared to baseline data placement strategies.
Percentage of variance in p99 latency explained by the number of 2-clusters in the system.
Time to compute data placements for a system with 600 servers and 6000 shards.

vldb2023-63.limiting_design_assumption: Balancing the aggregate query load across servers is sufficient to minimize query latency, particularly tail latency.
vldb2023-63.state_or_ir: A list of query shard sets and their frequencies, current shard locations, shard loads, and server capacities.
vldb2023-63.output: A new shard-to-server assignment map that minimizes query 'clustering' (a proxy for tail latency) while balancing load and minimizing data movement.
vldb2023-63.equation: Load-Balanced Placement + Fp + Cp + Mo -> PODP
vldb2023-63.principle_composition_rationale: The system uses Function Placement (Fp) to frame the problem as deciding where to place data shards. It then uses a Constraint-based (Cp) solver to find an optimal placement that maximizes a new parallelism metric. Finally, it applies Modularity (Mo) by partitioning the problem space to ensure the constraint-based approach remains tractable at large scale.
vldb2023-63.one_sentence_mechanism: Formulates data placement as a scalable mixed-integer linear program that minimizes 'clustering'—a proxy for resource contention and tail latency—by spreading the shards for each query across as many servers as possible.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces query parallelism as a first-class optimization objective for data placement, using a new metric called 'clustering' (the max number of a query's shards on any single server) and an MILP to minimize it.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The data placement algorithm is more complex (requires an MILP solver). It may also slightly increase the number of servers a query touches, but the paper finds the negative 'tail at scale' effect is small.

key_deltas.summary: Uses Partitioned Optimization Problems (POP) to make the MILP-based placement algorithm scalable to large clusters by breaking the global problem into smaller, independent sub-problems.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The resulting data placement is near-optimal rather than globally optimal, though experiments show the impact on query tail latency is negligible.

Fp.name: Function Placement
Fp.mechanism_trigger: The entire algorithm is designed to decide where to place data shards on physical servers.
Fp.why_it_applies: The core contribution is a new strategy for data placement. It places shards that are frequently queried together on different servers to maximize parallelism, directly manipulating the location of data to improve performance.

Cp.name: Constraint-based
Cp.mechanism_trigger: The use of a Mixed-Integer Linear Program (MILP) to find a data placement.
Cp.why_it_applies: The system formulates data placement as an optimization problem with an objective function (minimizing clustering) and explicit constraints for load balancing, memory capacity, and replication factor. This is a direct application of constraint-based solving.

Wa.name: Workload-Aware
Wa.mechanism_trigger: The MILP formulation takes as input the set of query shard sets and their frequencies.
Wa.why_it_applies: The placement decisions are directly driven by the observed query workload. The objective function explicitly minimizes the frequency-weighted sum of clustering for the given workload.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The identification of resource contention on individual servers as the cause of high tail latency.
Bo.why_it_applies: The paper's central argument is that co-locating a query's shards creates a performance bottleneck on a single server. The entire mechanism of maximizing parallelism is designed to alleviate this specific bottleneck.

Mo.name: Modularity
Mo.mechanism_trigger: The use of Partitioned Optimization Problems (POP) to break the global placement problem into smaller sub-problems.
Mo.why_it_applies: This mechanism structurally decomposes a single, large optimization problem into multiple smaller, independent modules (sub-problems) that can be solved separately. This decomposition is the key to achieving scalability.

Sc.name: Scalability
Sc.mechanism_trigger: The explicit goal and result of applying the POP technique.
Sc.why_it_applies: While often just a benefit, here the mechanism of partitioning is explicitly chosen to address the superlinear runtime scaling of the MILP solver, enabling the approach to work on large clusters. The design intent is scalability.

Ad.name: Adaptive Processing
Ad.mechanism_trigger: The 'sticky partitions' heuristic used to maintain partitions across rounds of data placement.
Ad.why_it_applies: The system adapts its partitioning strategy over time. To minimize data movement when workloads change, it greedily tries to keep shards in their previous partitions, adapting the assignment to maintain balance.

vldb2023-64.paper_title: Tigger: A Database Proxy That Bounces With User-Bypass
vldb2023-64.goal: To eliminate the overheads of traditional user-space database proxies by pushing DBMS-specific connection logic into the OS kernel using eBPF.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Reduction in transaction latency compared to other proxies.
Reduction in CPU utilization compared to other proxies.
Average transaction latency reduction for workload mirroring compared to Pgpool-II.
CPU utilization reduction for workload mirroring compared to Pgpool-II.

vldb2023-64.limiting_design_assumption: DBMS protocol logic (L7) must be processed in a user-space application, which requires repeated, expensive system calls and buffer copies between user-space and kernel-space to read from and write to network sockets.
vldb2023-64.state_or_ir: eBPF maps (stack, array, sockmap) to maintain state across events, such as idle sockets and connection state.
vldb2023-64.output: Redirected network packets between sockets within the kernel, or packets passed to user-space for complex/rare cases.
vldb2023-64.equation: User-space Proxy + Fp (Function Placement) + Cc (Common-Case Specialization) + Rc (Reuse of Computation) -> Tigger
vldb2023-64.principle_composition_rationale: The composition moves common-case (Cc) packet forwarding logic from user-space into the kernel (Fp) to eliminate system call overhead. For features like mirroring, this in-kernel placement enables efficient reuse (Rc) of prepared network buffers by cloning them, a primitive unavailable or inefficient from user-space.
vldb2023-64.one_sentence_mechanism: Tigger moves common-case database proxy logic for packet forwarding and mirroring from a user-space application into the Linux kernel using eBPF programs to avoid expensive system calls and buffer copies.

key_deltas.summary: Moves the common-case logic for forwarding query/result packets from a user-space application into kernel-space eBPF programs, avoiding system calls and buffer copies.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Increased implementation complexity due to eBPF's constraints (verifier, limited instruction set, stack size). Complex or rare operations (e.g., authentication, cancel requests) are punted to a slower user-space path.

key_deltas.summary: Implements workload mirroring by cloning and redirecting query packets to replica databases within the kernel, avoiding the user-space overhead of duplicating system calls and buffer copies for each replica.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Highly complex implementation involving multiple eBPF programs at different network stack layers (TC and socket) and header manipulation (TCP->UDP) to route packets within the kernel.

Fp.name: Function Placement
Fp.mechanism_trigger: The use of eBPF programs attached to kernel hooks (sockmaps) to execute DBMS protocol logic inside the kernel.
Fp.why_it_applies: The paper's central idea, 'user-bypass', is explicitly about pushing application logic from user-space into the kernel to be closer to the data (network buffers) and avoid crossing the user/kernel boundary.

Fp.name: Function Placement
Fp.mechanism_trigger: Using eBPF programs at the Traffic Control (TC) and socket layers to clone, rewrite, and redirect packets for workload mirroring.
Fp.why_it_applies: The logic for duplicating and forwarding traffic to replicas is moved from user-space into the kernel to avoid the overhead of multiple system calls and buffer copies.

Cc.name: Common-Case Specialization
Cc.mechanism_trigger: The hybrid design where eBPF handles the common case of query/result forwarding, while complex or infrequent operations like authentication and cancel requests are passed to a slower user-space component.
Cc.why_it_applies: The system creates a highly optimized 'fast path' for the most frequent operations, while retaining a more flexible but slower 'slow path' for exceptional cases, which is the definition of common-case specialization.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: The entire design is motivated by avoiding the performance cost of system calls and memory copies across the user-kernel boundary, which are fundamental aspects of the OS/CPU architecture.
Ha.why_it_applies: The design is aware of the system's software architecture and its performance characteristics, specifically the high cost of crossing the user/kernel protection boundary, and uses a modern OS feature (eBPF) to mitigate this cost.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The eBPF program at the TC layer clones the socket buffer destined for the primary database.
Rc.why_it_applies: Instead of the user-space application regenerating a packet for the replica, the kernel-prepared buffer for the primary is cloned and repurposed. The work of preparing the packet payload is done once and reused.

Al.name: Abstraction Lifting
Al.mechanism_trigger: The manual rewriting of packet headers at the TC layer, changing a TCP packet to a UDP packet, to route it from the egress path back up to an ingress path handler within the kernel.
Al.why_it_applies: The mechanism deliberately breaks the network layer abstractions to achieve its goal. It operates on the underlying packet structures directly instead of using the standard socket API, effectively lifting the abstraction to manipulate the implementation details.

vldb2023-65.paper_title: TreeLine: An Update-In-Place Key-Value Store for Modern Storage
vldb2023-65.problem_domain: Key-value store design for NVMe SSDs
vldb2023-65.goal: To develop a new update-in-place key-value store design for NVMe SSDs that has the read benefits of a classical update-in-place design while also mitigating its traditional write drawbacks, making it competitive with Log-Structured Merge Tree (LSM) designs.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Average throughput speedup over RocksDB on YCSB point workloads with 1024 byte records.
Average throughput speedup over LeanStore on YCSB point workloads with 1024 byte records.
