Pd.why_it_applies: The system uses probabilistic data structures to represent large sets of matches in a space-efficient manner, accepting a small, controllable false-positive rate to gain significant space and time efficiency.

Cm.name: Cost-based
Cm.mechanism_trigger: The filter utility model and the greedy selection algorithm (Algorithm 2).
Cm.why_it_applies: The system explicitly defines a utility model to estimate the 'pruning power' (benefit) of each filter. It then solves an optimization problem (max utility) to select the best set of filters, balancing benefit against the implicit cost of filter application overhead (by limiting k).

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The filter removal mechanism based on filter domination.
Bo.why_it_applies: The filter removal process is explicitly motivated by preventing the filter database size and selection time from becoming a bottleneck. The paper states that a growing database 'consumes much disk space' and 'harms the efficiency of filter selection'. The mechanism targets this emerging bottleneck.

vldb2023-112.paper_title: Computing Graph Edit Distance via Neural Graph Matching
vldb2023-112.goal: To design a machine learning framework that predicts not only the Graph Edit Distance (GED) value but also generates the corresponding interpretable edit path, improving upon models that treat GED as a pure regression task.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Decrease in Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on GED value prediction compared to the best existing GNN-based models.
Decrease in MAE on GED value (based on the generated edit path) compared to the state-of-the-art searching algorithm Noah.

vldb2023-112.baseline_name: GNN-based GED regression models (e.g., SimGNN, TaGSim)
vldb2023-112.limiting_design_assumption: Efficient, learning-based GED computation must treat the problem as a pure regression task, mapping a graph pair to a single scalar similarity score, thereby forgoing the generation of an explicit edit path.
vldb2023-112.state_or_ir: A soft |V1| x |V2| matching matrix (C_match) and a cost matrix (C_cost) representing node-to-node correspondence and edit costs.
vldb2023-112.output: A predicted GED value and a concrete graph edit path.
vldb2023-112.equation: GNN-based GED Regression + La + Se + Ep -> GEDGNN
vldb2023-112.principle_composition_rationale: The composition transforms a black-box regression task into an interpretable, two-stage process. La provides the core learning engine to approximate the intractable GED problem. Se forces the learned model to produce a structured, meaningful intermediate representation (the matching matrix) instead of an opaque scalar. This explicit representation is the key that enables Ep to reframe the path-finding problem into a tractable, classical graph algorithm (k-best matching), bridging the gap between the learned continuous space and the discrete solution space.
vldb2023-112.one_sentence_mechanism: Replaces direct regression to a scalar Graph Edit Distance (GED) value with a two-stage process where a GNN first learns a soft node-matching matrix, which is then decoded into a high-quality edit path using a k-best bipartite matching algorithm.

key_deltas.summary: Instead of regressing directly to a scalar GED value, the model jointly predicts the GED value and a soft node-to-node matching matrix that explicitly represents the correspondence between the two graphs.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Increased model complexity and computational cost (O(n^2)) during inference compared to baseline regression models (O(n+m)).

key_deltas.summary: A post-processing algorithm recovers a high-quality, discrete edit path from the GNN's soft matching matrix by framing the problem as a k-best weighted bipartite matching search.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Adds a significant post-processing step with O(k*n^3) time complexity, making it slower than pure GNN inference, but faster than exponential search baselines like Noah.

La.name: Learned Approximation
La.mechanism_trigger: The use of a GNN-based model (GEDGNN) to predict both the GED value and the matching matrix.
La.why_it_applies: The entire framework is a learned model that approximates the NP-hard function for computing graph edit distance and the optimal node matching. It replaces an exact, exponential-time search with a fast, learned inference process.

Se.name: Semantically Explicit
Se.mechanism_trigger: The model's output of a matching matrix, instead of only a final scalar GED value.
Se.why_it_applies: Unlike baselines that treat GED as a black-box regression, this work makes the intermediate reasoning explicit by producing a matching matrix. This matrix has a clear semantic meaning—the correspondence between nodes—and is the crucial component that enables the generation of an edit path in the next stage.

Ep.name: Equivalence-based
Ep.mechanism_trigger: The post-processing algorithm that converts the task of finding a good edit path into a weighted bipartite matching problem.
Ep.why_it_applies: The paper explicitly reframes the problem of extracting the ground-truth matching from the learned matrix into an equivalent, and more tractable, maximum weight bipartite matching problem. The k-best algorithm then explores multiple high-quality candidate solutions within this reframed problem space.

Cm.name: Cost-based
Cm.mechanism_trigger: The use of a GED lower bound to prune the search space of the k-best matching algorithm.
Cm.why_it_applies: The improved k-best matching framework (Algorithm 2) uses a cost-based heuristic (the GED lower bound) to prune subspaces that are unlikely to yield a better solution than the current best path. This is a cost-based optimization of the search process.

vldb2023-113.problem_domain: Approximate K-nearest-neighbor graph construction over ranged subsets of data
vldb2023-113.goal: To build an index that takes a search key range as the query and returns the KGraph of vectors whose search keys are within the query range, while addressing the challenge of huge index size.

concrete_numbers.number_or_delta: O(K n^3) to O(K n log n)
concrete_numbers.meaning: Reduction in average-case index size complexity from the raw brute-force approach to the optimized ARKGraph index.
Query performance improvement compared to the baseline method of building a KGraph on-the-fly for vectors in the query range.
Index size reduction for ARKGraph with delta compression (0.72GB) compared to without (20GB) on the DEEP1B dataset with K=64.

vldb2023-113.baseline_name: Brute-force raw ARKGraph index / On-the-fly KGraph construction
vldb2023-113.limiting_design_assumption: A K-Nearest-Neighbor graph for a specific key range must be either fully materialized in an index for every possible range or computed from scratch at query time.
vldb2023-113.state_or_ir: An index storing compact adjacent lists, which group ranges with identical KNNs, and delta-compressed lists of KNNs for consecutive partial ranges.
vldb2023-113.output: An index that can quickly reconstruct the KGraph for any given key range.
vldb2023-113.principle_composition_rationale: The system first decomposes (Mo) the problem of finding KNNs in a full range into two independent partial-range subproblems, reducing the problem space from quadratic to linear. Within this new structure, the system aggressively reuses computation (Rc) and avoids redundant work (Wv) by identifying large sets of ranges that share identical KNN lists and by storing only the deltas between consecutive lists. This composition transforms a problem with prohibitive storage complexity into a tractable one.
vldb2023-113.one_sentence_mechanism: Reduces the prohibitive storage cost of pre-calculating K-Nearest-Neighbor graphs for all possible data sub-ranges by decomposing ranges into mergeable partial ranges and using compact representations and delta compression to exploit redundancy.

key_deltas.summary: Instead of storing a KNN list for every possible range, the system groups ranges where a vector's KNNs are identical into a single 'compact adjacent list', drastically reducing storage redundancy.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Increased complexity in index construction and query processing, which now requires a lookup structure (e.g., 2D segment tree) to find the correct compact range for a query.

key_deltas.summary: Decomposes the problem of finding KNNs for full ranges [x,y] into finding KNNs for two 'partial ranges' [x, i) and (i, y] for each vector v_i. It then applies delta compression to store only the changes between KNN lists of consecutive partial ranges.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Query processing requires fetching two partial KNN lists and merging them. With delta compression, it also requires a decompression step, which adds latency.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance: skip work that cannot alter externally observable results
Wv.mechanism_trigger: The definition of a 'compact adjacent list' which groups all ranges [x,y] that share the exact same KNN list.
Wv.why_it_applies: The mechanism avoids the storage work of materializing countless redundant copies of the same KNN list. This is result-preserving because the query result for any range within the 'compact range' is guaranteed to be identical.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: Storing the KNN list only once for a 'compact range'.
Rc.why_it_applies: A single computed KNN list is stored and then reused to answer all queries whose range falls within the corresponding compact range, avoiding re-storage and re-computation.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The delta compression mechanism, which stores only the changes (one insertion, one deletion) between the KNN lists of consecutive compact partial ranges.
Rc.why_it_applies: Instead of storing each new KNN list from scratch, the system reuses the previous list and applies a small, stored transformation (the delta). This is a direct form of reusing a prior computed artifact.

Ig.name: Invariant-Guided
Ig.mechanism_trigger: The five conditions in Definition 4 that define the maximal boundaries of a compact range.
Ig.why_it_applies: The algorithm is guided by the invariant that the KNN list remains unchanged within certain range boundaries. The mechanism is designed to find the largest possible regions where this invariant holds to maximize storage savings.

Mo.name: Modularity
Mo.mechanism_trigger: The observation that 'the KNN of v_i in any range [x,y] \ {i} can be derived from the KNN of v_i in the 'partial range' [x, i) and the KNN of v_i in the 'partial range' (i, y]''.
Mo.why_it_applies: The mechanism decomposes the problem of finding KNNs in a 2D range space (defined by start and end points) into two independent 1D 'partial range' subproblems. This structural decomposition is the key to reducing the problem complexity from O(n^2) to O(n).

Co.name: Composability
Co.mechanism_trigger: At query time, the system fetches the two KNN lists from the partial ranges and merges them to find the top-K neighbors for the full query range.
Co.why_it_applies: This is the necessary counterpart to Modularity. The system is designed such that the results of the independent subproblems (partial range KNNs) can be composed to correctly solve the original problem (full range KNNs).

vldb2023-114.goal: To generalize stable coloring to an approximate version, called quasi-stable coloring, to achieve much higher graph compression ratios for dimensionality reduction in downstream applications like linear programming, max-flow, and centrality computation.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Problem size reduction for the qap15 linear program, reducing solution time from 22 minutes to 17 seconds with only 5% error.
The size of the reduced graph relative to the original when using baseline stable coloring, indicating poor compression.
Compression ratio on OpenFlights dataset with q-stable coloring, compared to 1.29:1 for stable coloring.
Average runtime improvement for betweenness centrality approximation compared to prior sampling-based work.

vldb2023-114.limiting_design_assumption: Vertices can only be grouped into the same color class if they have strictly identical neighborhood structures (i.e., the exact same number of neighbors for each other color). This reliance on strict symmetry makes it ineffective for real-world graphs which are not perfectly regular, leading to poor compression and brittleness to small changes.
vldb2023-114.state_or_ir: A partition of graph vertices into color classes.
vldb2023-114.output: A compressed (reduced) graph where nodes represent colors, which is then used to compute an approximate solution for a given problem (e.g., LP, max-flow).
vldb2023-114.equation: Strict Symmetry (Stable Coloring) + Cr + Bo -> Quasi-Stable Coloring
vldb2023-114.principle_composition_rationale: The composition enables a practical approximate graph compression scheme. Consistency Relaxation (Cr) provides the theoretical opening by defining a lossy but more compressive notion of vertex equivalence. However, this relaxation makes finding the best coloring NP-hard. Bottleneck-Oriented (Bo) design provides the practical algorithm to navigate this hard search space by heuristically and greedily fixing the largest source of error at each step.
vldb2023-114.one_sentence_mechanism: Relaxes the strict symmetry requirement of stable coloring to an approximate, error-bounded version (ε-stable) and uses a heuristic, bottleneck-oriented refinement algorithm to find a good coloring that trades compression for accuracy in downstream tasks.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces ε-stable coloring, which relaxes the strict equality condition of stable coloring to an approximate one, allowing vertices with similar (but not identical) neighborhood counts to be grouped together.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The resulting compressed representation is lossy, introducing approximation errors in downstream applications. The quality of the approximation is traded for the degree of compression.

key_deltas.summary: Proposes a practical, heuristic-based algorithm (Rothko) to compute a good quasi-stable coloring, as finding a maximal coloring is proven to be NP-hard.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The algorithm is a heuristic and is not guaranteed to find a maximal quasi-stable coloring. The quality of the found coloring depends on the heuristics used for splitting and the runtime budget.

Cr.name: Consistency Relaxation
Cr.mechanism_trigger: The definition of ε-stable coloring, which replaces the strict equality constraint of stable coloring (`count(v1, Cj) == count(v2, Cj)`) with a bounded difference (`|count(v1, Cj) - count(v2, Cj)| <= ε`).
Cr.why_it_applies: This mechanism deliberately relaxes a structural consistency property (vertex equivalence based on neighborhood structure) to gain performance (compression, speed) at the cost of exactness. The paper explicitly frames this as a 'relaxation of stable coloring'.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The Rothko algorithm's core loop, which identifies the 'witness': the pair of color partitions with the maximum error (degree difference).
Bo.why_it_applies: The algorithm's strategy is to iteratively find the largest source of approximation error in the current coloring—the bottleneck to improving quality—and refine the partition specifically to reduce that error. This directly matches the principle of targeting the primary limiter.

Ad.name: Adaptive Processing
Ad.mechanism_trigger: The iterative nature of the Rothko algorithm and its stopping conditions.
Ad.why_it_applies: The algorithm is explicitly designed as an 'anytime' algorithm. It can be stopped at any point to yield a valid coloring, and the quality of the coloring (and thus the downstream approximation) adapts to the amount of computation time allowed, controlled by a target number of colors or a maximum error.

vldb2023-115.paper_title: Temporal SIR-GN: Efficient and Effective Structural Representation Learning for Temporal Graphs
vldb2023-115.problem_domain: Structural node representation learning for temporal graphs
vldb2023-115.goal: To generate a single, unsupervised embedding for every node that encodes the temporal evolution of that node's structural role, with higher efficiency and effectiveness than existing methods.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Accuracy and F1 score on the noise-free Synth0.0 dataset.
Improvement in space complexity compared to state-of-the-art temporal GNNs, which require O(|T| x |V| x h) space.
Runtime speedup compared to some competitor methods.
Training time on the GDELT dataset, where competitors could not run on the available hardware.

vldb2023-115.limiting_design_assumption: Generating temporal node embeddings requires a diachronic approach: first, materialize an intermediate embedding for every node at every timestamp, and then aggregate these per-timestamp embeddings using computationally expensive sequence-learning models (e.g., RNNs, LSTMs).
vldb2023-115.state_or_ir: The matrix of node representations R from the previous iteration, and derived 'description vectors' D representing cluster membership probabilities.
vldb2023-115.output: A final matrix R of node embeddings of dimensionality |V| x (c^2 + c).
vldb2023-115.equation: Temporal GNNs (Diachronic) + Wv + Ep -> Temporal SIR-GN
vldb2023-115.principle_composition_rationale: This composition achieves scalable structural embedding by fundamentally changing the computation model. Wv enables efficiency by avoiding the baseline's expensive materialization of per-timestamp state. Ep provides the semantic foundation, ensuring that this efficient, direct computation correctly captures the desired property of structural equivalence, inspired by the WL isomorphism test.
vldb2023-115.one_sentence_mechanism: The system computes structural node embeddings for temporal graphs by iteratively clustering node representations and aggregating neighborhood information across all timestamps into a single summary of cluster frequencies and transitions, using a linear-time factorization to avoid quadratic complexity.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces the diachronic, materialize-then-aggregate GNN approach with a non-diachronic, iterative summarization method that directly computes a single embedding per node, avoiding massive intermediate state.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The method is unsupervised and does not use node features. The embedding dimensionality is coupled with the number of clusters `c`, a hyperparameter.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a linear-time temporal aggregation algorithm by factorizing the computation of a cluster transition matrix, which would naively require quadratic time in the number of timestamps.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The aggregation formula is specific to capturing first-order transitions between neighborhood clusters and may not capture more complex temporal patterns. It introduces a hyperparameter `alpha` to modulate the temporal decay effect.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance: skip work that cannot alter externally observable results
Wv.mechanism_trigger: The adoption of a 'non-diachronic objective' for computing embeddings.
Wv.why_it_applies: The system entirely avoids the work of computing and materializing an intermediate embedding for every node at every timestamp. This avoided work does not change the final result (a single embedding per node), but dramatically reduces computation and memory, forming the central efficiency argument against the baseline.

Ep.name: Equivalence-based
Ep.mechanism_trigger: The algorithm's design emulates the Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL) isomorphism test.
Ep.why_it_applies: The goal is to assign similar embeddings to nodes with similar structural roles, formalized as temporal automorphism. The WL-inspired iterative aggregation is a mechanism to compute representations that preserve this structural equivalence. The paper proves that temporally-automorphic nodes receive identical embeddings.

Ep.name: Equivalence-based
Ep.mechanism_trigger: The derivation of a linear-time recurrence (Lemmas 3.8, 3.9) to compute the cluster transition matrix.
Ep.why_it_applies: The mechanism replaces a quadratic-time computation with an equivalent linear-time one. This is a form of equivalence-based optimization, where a computational plan (the naive nested loop) is rewritten into a more efficient but semantically identical plan (the linear scan using the auxiliary vector Z). The paper explicitly states this is done 'without losing exactness'.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The iterative structure of the main loop in Algorithm 1.
Rc.why_it_applies: Each iteration `i+1` reuses the node representations `R_i` computed in the previous iteration as the basis for the next round of clustering and aggregation. This iterative refinement reuses prior computation to explore larger neighborhood structures.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The focus on optimizing the temporal aggregation step.
Bo.why_it_applies: The paper identifies the naive quadratic computation of the cluster transition matrix as a key performance bottleneck that could lead to 'unaffordable running time'. The factorization is a targeted optimization to relieve this specific bottleneck.

vldb2023-116.paper_title: Scaling Up Structural Clustering to Large Probabilistic Graphs Using Lyapunov Central Limit Theorem
vldb2023-116.goal: To scale up structural clustering to large probabilistic graphs by replacing the bottleneck computation of structural similarity probability with an efficient statistical approximation.

concrete_numbers.number_or_delta: up to three orders of magnitude
concrete_numbers.meaning: Improvement in runtime over the exact DP calculation on datasets the baseline can handle.
Speedup of NUSCAN over USCAN on the cnr-2000 dataset.
NUSCAN can cluster the arabic-2005 graph with over half a billion edges in less than an hour.
The baseline, USCAN, was unable to complete on datasets with more than 30 million edges.

vldb2023-116.limiting_design_assumption: The probability of structural similarity for a pair of vertices must be computed exactly over all possible worlds. This necessitates a Dynamic Programming (DP) algorithm with quadratic runtime complexity, which becomes a bottleneck for vertices with high degrees.
vldb2023-116.state_or_ir: A probabilistic graph G = (V, E, p), and intermediate random variable representations for structural similarity.
vldb2023-116.output: A set of reliable clusters, hubs, and outliers.
vldb2023-116.equation: Exact DP Calculation + Bo + Pd -> NUSCAN
vldb2023-116.principle_composition_rationale: The design is bottleneck-oriented (Bo), identifying the exact probability calculation as the performance limiter. It then applies probabilistic design (Pd) to replace this bottleneck with a fast, asymptotically-correct approximation using the Central Limit Theorem, dramatically improving performance and scalability.
vldb2023-116.one_sentence_mechanism: Replaces the bottleneck quadratic-time exact calculation of structural similarity in probabilistic graphs with a linear-time approximation based on the Lyapunov Central Limit Theorem for nodes with large neighborhoods.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces the exact, quadratic-time Dynamic Programming calculation of structural similarity probability with a linear-time approximation based on the Lyapunov Central Limit Theorem for vertex pairs with large combined neighborhoods.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The computed probability is an approximation, not exact. However, experiments show the resulting clusters are 'practically identical' or in 'near perfect agreement' with the exact method.

key_deltas.summary: Not specified in provided text
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Not specified in provided text

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The entire algorithm is designed to replace the specific DP calculation for structural similarity probability.
Bo.why_it_applies: The paper explicitly identifies the quadratic-time DP calculation as the 'main bottleneck step' and 'the bottleneck process' and designs the CLT approximation specifically to alleviate it.

Pd.name: Probabilistic Design
Pd.mechanism_trigger: The use of Lyapunov's Central Limit Theorem to approximate a probability distribution.
Pd.why_it_applies: The core mechanism recasts the problem of computing a probability over an exponential number of 'possible worlds' into an approximation by expressing the structural similarity as a sum of random variables and invoking a statistical limit theorem to approximate its distribution with a Normal distribution.

Cc.name: Common-Case Specialization
Cc.mechanism_trigger: The hybrid approach of using CLT for large neighborhoods and DP for small ones, based on a threshold 't'.
Cc.why_it_applies: The system uses two different algorithms for the same logical task, specializing the fast approximation for the expensive case (large neighborhoods) and using the exact algorithm for the cheap case (small neighborhoods).

vldb2023-117.paper_title: Efficient Maximum k-Plex Computation over Large Sparse Graphs
vldb2023-117.goal: To design a new exact and efficient algorithm for computing a maximum k-plex in a large sparse graph, outperforming existing state-of-the-art algorithms.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Speedup of kPlexS over the best of three baseline algorithms (KpLeX, Maplex, BnB-ct, BnB) on the socfb-Texas84 graph for k=5.
kPlexS with a time limit of 50s solves more instances on the real-world graph collection than prior art with a time limit of 1800s.

vldb2023-117.baseline_name: Two-stage branch-and-bound search (e.g., BnB, Maplex, KpLeX)
vldb2023-117.limiting_design_assumption: The most efficient way to find a maximum k-plex is to first perform a global graph reduction and then apply a single, global branch-and-bound search to the entire reduced graph using only first-order (vertex degree) pruning information.
vldb2023-117.state_or_ir: Graph representation (adjacency list for the global graph, adjacency matrix for dense subgraphs).
vldb2023-117.output: A maximum k-plex, represented as a set of vertices.
vldb2023-117.equation: Two-stage Global Search + Mo + Cc + Ig -> kPlexS
vldb2023-117.principle_composition_rationale: The design's effectiveness comes from the synergy between decomposition and specialization. Modularity (Mo) breaks the monolithic search problem into many small, local subproblems. This decomposition enables Common-Case Specialization (Cc), creating a specialized solver (BBMatrix) that can afford powerful but expensive techniques (like using second-order information) precisely because the subproblems are small and dense. Invariant-Guided (Ig) design provides the logic for the powerful pruning within this specialized solver, using necessary properties of k-plexes to aggressively shrink the search space.
vldb2023-117.one_sentence_mechanism: Decomposes the global search for a maximum k-plex into a series of local branch-and-bound searches on small, dense subgraphs, enabling the use of powerful but otherwise expensive second-order pruning techniques.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces the single, global branch-and-bound search on a large sparse graph with an iterative framework that solves a series of smaller branch-and-bound problems on extracted dense subgraphs.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Introduces an outer loop over vertices, but each subproblem is much smaller, enabling more powerful but computationally intensive pruning techniques that would be infeasible on the global graph.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces more powerful and efficient graph reduction techniques, both in preprocessing and during the branch-and-bound search, by using second-order information (common neighbors).
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The second-order techniques in BBMatrix require quadratic space for an adjacency matrix and common neighbor counts, making them suitable only for the small, dense subgraphs generated by the Δ1 framework.

Mo.name: Modularity
Mo.mechanism_trigger: The framework's main loop which, instead of solving one large problem, iteratively picks a vertex `u` and solves for the maximum k-plex containing `u`.
Mo.why_it_applies: The system decomposes the single, global search problem into a series of smaller, independent, and more manageable subproblems, each localized to a vertex's 2-hop neighborhood.

Cc.name: Common-Case Specialization
Cc.mechanism_trigger: The development of the BBMatrix algorithm, which is specifically designed to run on the small, dense subgraphs extracted by the main framework.
Cc.why_it_applies: The system identifies a common subproblem structure (small, dense graphs) and develops a specialized algorithm (BBMatrix) for it, which uses techniques (adjacency matrix, second-order info) that would be inefficient on the general case (large, sparse graphs).

Lo.name: Locality of Reference
Lo.mechanism_trigger: Restricting the search for a k-plex containing vertex `u` to the subgraph induced by its 2-hop neighborhood, N≤2G(u).
Lo.why_it_applies: The algorithm exploits the fact that a connected k-plex is a local structure. By restricting the search to a local neighborhood, it improves efficiency.

Ig.name: Invariant-Guided
Ig.mechanism_trigger: The use of core pruning, truss pruning, and the second-order reduction rules (RR4, RR5) and upper bounds (UB2).
Ig.why_it_applies: All pruning and reduction rules are derived from necessary properties (invariants) that any valid k-plex of a certain size must satisfy. For example, Lemma 4.2 states any edge in a k-plex must participate in a minimum number of triangles. The algorithm prunes any part of the search space that violates these invariants.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: The explicit choice to represent the small, dense subgraphs in BBMatrix using an adjacency matrix.
Ha.why_it_applies: The choice of data structure is explicitly justified by the properties of the data (small and dense), which makes the quadratic space of an adjacency matrix acceptable and subsequent operations like checking adjacency and common neighbors (which access memory differently than adjacency lists) efficient.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The incremental maintenance and use of common neighbor counts (cn(u,v)) within the BBMatrix algorithm.
Rc.why_it_applies: The common neighbor counts are computed once and then incrementally updated as the search progresses. These maintained values are reused for multiple different second-order pruning checks (RR4, RR5, UB2), avoiding recomputation.

vldb2023-118.paper_title: Text Indexing for Long Patterns: Anchors are All you Need
vldb2023-118.goal: To design a text index that offers good performance across four key measures simultaneously: index space, query time, construction space, and construction time, particularly for applications with long query patterns.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Query time improvement compared to the suffix array.

concrete_numbers.number_or_delta: up to 2 orders of magnitude less
concrete_numbers.meaning: Index space reduction compared to the suffix array.
Index space reduction for ℓ=1024 compared to the FM-index.
Query time on the full human genome for ℓ=2^14 compared to the FM-index, while using 16MB of space versus >1GB.

vldb2023-118.baseline_name: Classic text indexes (suffix array, compressed suffix array, FM-index)
vldb2023-118.limiting_design_assumption: A text index must index all suffixes of the text to answer queries for patterns of any arbitrary length. This forces a trade-off where improving one performance measure (e.g., space via compression) degrades another (e.g., query time).
vldb2023-118.state_or_ir: The set of reduced bd-anchors A_ℓ,r(S), two compacted tries built on suffixes/prefixes starting at anchors (T_R, T_L), and a 2D range reporting data structure.
vldb2023-118.output: The set of all starting positions in the text where a given query pattern occurs.
vldb2023-118.equation: Full Suffix Indexing + Cc (Common-Case Specialization) + Wv (Work Avoidance) + Ha (Hardware-Aware) -> BDA-index
vldb2023-118.principle_composition_rationale: The composition enables a shift from exhaustive indexing to specialized, sparse indexing. `Cc` first restricts the problem domain to long patterns, which creates the opportunity for `Wv` to safely avoid indexing most text positions. `Ha` then makes the construction of this sparse index practical by using a memory-hierarchy-aware algorithm to keep the peak memory footprint small, aligned with the final small index size.
vldb2023-118.one_sentence_mechanism: Replaces exhaustive suffix indexing with a sparse index built only on a small set of 'bidirectional anchors', which is sufficient to find all occurrences of long patterns while dramatically reducing space and improving query time.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces indexing all suffixes with indexing a sparse, locally consistent set of 'bd-anchors', which is sufficient for finding all occurrences of patterns longer than a given length ℓ.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The index is specialized and only guarantees correctness for patterns of length |P| ≥ ℓ. A new parameter, ℓ, must be chosen at construction time.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a fast, average-case linear-time algorithm to compute bd-anchors and a semi-external-memory algorithm to build the index, avoiding the Θ(n) construction space of prior methods.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The construction algorithm's O(n) time complexity is on average, with a worst-case of O(nℓ). The construction relies on external memory (disk), which can be slower than a pure in-memory build if sufficient RAM is available.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance
Wv.mechanism_trigger: Indexing only the O(n/ℓ) bd-anchor positions instead of all n suffix positions.
Wv.why_it_applies: For the specified query class (patterns of length |P| ≥ ℓ), the locally consistent anchor property guarantees that all occurrences can be found from the sparse set of anchors. Therefore, the work of indexing the remaining n - O(n/ℓ) positions is proven to not alter the externally observable query results.

Cc.name: Common-Case Specialization
Cc.mechanism_trigger: The entire index design is predicated on having a lower bound ℓ on the length of query patterns.
Cc.why_it_applies: The system specializes for the common case of long patterns, explicitly trading away the ability to handle arbitrarily short patterns for significant improvements in index size, construction space, and query time.

Pd.name: Probabilistic Design
Pd.mechanism_trigger: The analysis of the index's space complexity relies on the expected number of bd-anchors.
Pd.why_it_applies: The design's practicality and space efficiency guarantees are justified by probabilistic analysis showing the number of anchors is expected to be small (O(n/ℓ)) on random strings, which is validated empirically on real data.

Pd.name: Probabilistic Design
Pd.mechanism_trigger: The fast bd-anchor computation algorithm's performance analysis relies on the expected number of minimizers.
Pd.why_it_applies: The algorithm's average-case O(n) complexity is a direct result of the expected number of minimizers being small, which limits the number of candidate comparisons required.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: The semi-external-memory index construction algorithm.
Ha.why_it_applies: The algorithm is explicitly designed to be aware of the memory hierarchy (RAM vs. disk). It processes the text in small blocks to fit in RAM, and then sequentially scans large intermediate structures (the full suffix array) from disk to keep peak memory usage low, matching the small final index size.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: Using a pre-built LCP data structure to answer LCE queries in O(1) time during the computation of bd-anchors.
Rc.why_it_applies: Instead of repeatedly comparing characters to find the lexicographically smallest rotation of a substring, the algorithm reuses the pre-computed LCP information to perform these comparisons in constant time, which is key to its efficiency.

vldb2023-119.goal: To determine, for all potential input documents, whether the extracted materialized view can be kept consistent with the updated source documents without running the extractor after updating the documents in the database.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Maximum verification time per primitive extractor, which is independent of database size.
Time required for full re-extraction on the DBLP corpus.
Estimated time for just the `diff` step of an incremental update baseline on the DBLP corpus.

vldb2023-119.limiting_design_assumption: Updates to source documents require re-running the extractor, either on the full document or on changed portions of it, to maintain the materialized view of extracted relations.
vldb2023-119.state_or_ir: Regex with capture variables, vset-automata, eVset-automata
vldb2023-119.output: A boolean decision on whether an update is 'pseudo-irrelevant' with respect to an extractor.
vldb2023-119.equation: Re-extraction on update + Fs + Is + Wv -> Autonomous View Maintenance
vldb2023-119.principle_composition_rationale: The system leverages a Formal Specification (Fs) of extractors and updates to prove that they are non-interfering, achieving Isolation for Correctness (Is). This proof enables Work Avoidance (Wv) by skipping the expensive re-extraction process entirely, replacing it with a simple, fast update.
vldb2023-119.one_sentence_mechanism: Uses static analysis on the formal automata of information extractors and update expressions to prove non-interference, which allows it to avoid expensive re-extraction by simply shifting the offsets of previously extracted data spans.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a static verifier that analyzes the formal specifications of an update and an extractor to prove that re-extraction is unnecessary.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The verifier has a non-zero runtime cost (polynomial time, up to 3.5 minutes in experiments). The analysis is sound but not complete, so it may conservatively classify some safe updates as requiring re-extraction (false negatives).

key_deltas.summary: If an update is verified as pseudo-irrelevant, the system updates the extracted span relation by algorithmically shifting span offsets without re-parsing the document.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: This mechanism is only applicable for the class of updates proven to be pseudo-irrelevant by the verifier.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance
Wv.mechanism_trigger: The static verifier successfully proves that an update is pseudo-irrelevant.
Wv.why_it_applies: The system avoids the high cost of re-extraction because the static analysis proves that this work cannot alter the extracted content, only its position (span offsets). The avoided work is guaranteed not to change the semantic result.

Fs.name: Formal Specification
Fs.mechanism_trigger: The entire verification process operates on formal models of extractors and updates.
Fs.why_it_applies: Extractors and updates are defined as 'regex with capture variables' and are converted to vset-automata. The analysis of their properties (like non-overlap) is performed on these formal, executable specifications, not on imperative code or data instances.

Is.name: Isolation for Correctness
Is.mechanism_trigger: The 'independence' test performed by the verifier.
Is.why_it_applies: The verifier explicitly checks that the regions affected by an update are disjoint from the regions (content and context) critical to an extractor. This isolation is the key property that guarantees the correctness of avoiding re-extraction.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The 'Shift a span' algorithm is executed after a successful verification.
Rc.why_it_applies: The system reuses the previously computed extracted spans (the result of the original extraction) and applies a simple, inexpensive transformation (shifting offsets) to produce the new extracted relation, avoiding the need to re-compute it from the source document.

vldb2023-120.paper_title: REmatch: a novel regex engine for finding all matches
vldb2023-120.problem_domain: Regular expression matching and information extraction
vldb2023-120.goal: To design and implement a regular expression engine that supports 'all-match semantics' (finding all, including overlapping, matches) with performance comparable to traditional engines that only find leftmost-longest matches.

concrete_numbers.meaning: The full REmatch system with all optimizations is several orders of magnitude faster than a naive implementation of the all-match evaluation algorithm.
On the DNA dataset, the Node Manager optimization reduced average memory usage from 1202.1 MB in the Naive version to 3.19 MB.
On the DNA dataset, REmatch finds an average of 16,187.4 outputs, while an engine like RE2 that does not support all-match semantics finds only 10,556.9.

vldb2023-120.limiting_design_assumption: The standard 'leftmost-longest' matching paradigm is sufficient. This means engines find the match starting at the earliest possible position, and make it as long as possible, then resume searching after the match ends. This assumption prevents the discovery of overlapping matches.
vldb2023-120.state_or_ir: REQL (query language), logical VA (logical plan), extended VA (eVA, physical plan), Enumerable Compact Set (ECS, output data structure).
vldb2023-120.output: The set of all mappings from capture variables to document spans, including overlapping matches.
vldb2023-120.equation: Leftmost-Longest Matching + Se + Rc + Wv -> REmatch
vldb2023-120.principle_composition_rationale: The shift to an 'all-match' semantic (Se) creates two new challenges: representing a potentially huge output set, and the computational cost of finding it. These are solved by the other two principles. A compact, shared DAG representation reuses sub-solutions (Rc) to make the output space manageable, while aggressive, provably-correct work avoidance (Wv) prunes the search space to make the computation practical.
vldb2023-120.one_sentence_mechanism: REmatch replaces traditional leftmost-longest regex matching with a single-pass enumeration algorithm over a determinized automaton that builds a compact, shared representation of all possible matches, made practical through aggressive filtering and memory management optimizations.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces the standard 'leftmost-longest' matching semantic with an 'all-match' semantic, using a single-pass enumeration algorithm that constructs a compact graph representation of all possible matches.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The algorithm is inherently more complex and requires sophisticated memory management (the ECS and its Node Manager) to handle the potentially large output space without prohibitive memory or time costs.

key_deltas.summary: Implements a suite of performance optimizations, including a pre-filtering pass and automaton-level rewrites, to make the all-match enumeration algorithm practical and competitive with traditional engines.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Increased implementation complexity. The Light Search adds a preliminary pass over the data, and the Next Index trades increased memory space for faster lookups.

Se.name: Semantically Explicit
Se.mechanism_trigger: The introduction of the REQL language and its 'all-match' semantics, which formally defines the output as the set of all possible mappings.
Se.why_it_applies: The paper's primary contribution is to replace the implicit, procedural 'leftmost-longest' semantics of traditional regex with an explicit, declarative 'all-match' semantics. This allows users to express queries for all occurrences, including overlapping ones, which was previously difficult or impossible.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The use of the Enumerable Compact Set (ECS), a DAG data structure, to represent the set of all output sequences.
Rc.why_it_applies: The ECS avoids redundant representation of matches by sharing common prefixes and suffixes of output sequences. For instance, two matches that differ only in their final part will share the same path through the DAG until the point of divergence. This sharing is a direct form of reusing the representation of a computed sub-result.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance: skip work that cannot alter externally observable results
Wv.mechanism_trigger: The 'Light Search' filtering module and the 'Offsets' optimization.
Wv.why_it_applies: Light Search identifies and skips full evaluation on document segments where no matches can possibly occur. This avoided work is guaranteed not to change the final set of outputs. Similarly, the Offsets optimization avoids creating and tracking state for variable captures in partial matches that are guaranteed to fail later. This is state management work that cannot affect the final result.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The 'Node Manager' for the ECS data structure.
Bo.why_it_applies: The paper identifies that naively building the ECS with standard memory allocation is 'highly inefficient'. The Node Manager, with its memory pooling and garbage collection, is a targeted optimization to relieve this specific memory management bottleneck.

Cc.name: Common-Case Specialization
Cc.mechanism_trigger: The 'Next Index' optimization.
Cc.why_it_applies: The Next Index uses a 128-entry array to speed up state transitions for ASCII documents. This specializes the transition mechanism for the common case of ASCII character sets, trading space for faster, direct-indexed lookups, which is more efficient than a generic map-based approach.

vldb2023-121.goal: To automatically extract structured data records from modern (Web 2.0) web pages that feature heterogeneous schemas and nested, nonlinear structures, which violate the assumptions of traditional extraction methods.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Miria's F1-score on the COMMENT dataset, outperforming the SOTA PROSE system which scored 0.82.
On the GOOGLE dataset with nested records, Miria (with Encode_STR) achieves 0.93 recall, while a traditional baseline (DEPTA) achieves only 0.30 recall.

vldb2023-121.limiting_design_assumption: Web records are assumed to have (1) a uniform schema and (2) a linear structure. This implies that records are encoded in HTML using very similar tag tree structures, which can be found by measuring similarity on tag paths or subtrees.
vldb2023-121.state_or_ir: Node Encoding Sequence (NES), an integer sequence representing the flattened DOM tree.
vldb2023-121.output: A set of subtrees from the original DOM tree, each corresponding to an extracted web record.
vldb2023-121.equation: Similarity-based Extraction + Ig + Lu + Gr -> Miria
vldb2023-121.principle_composition_rationale: The composition replaces a brittle, holistic comparison with a robust, bottom-up reconstruction. The design is generalized (Gr) to handle modern web pages by searching for local structural invariants (Ig). This search is made possible by first transforming the tree-structured problem into a homogeneous sequential domain (Lu) where efficient pattern mining algorithms can be applied.
vldb2023-121.one_sentence_mechanism: Miria transforms a web page's DOM tree into a sequence to find structurally invariant subtrees via frequent pattern mining, then uses these subtrees as anchors to reconstruct full web records through a bottom-up path matching process, making it robust to nested and heterogeneous record structures.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces ancestor-dependent node encoding (tag paths) with a position-independent structural encoding, making record identification robust to nested structures.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The structural encoding is more computationally complex than simple tag path generation, although it remains linear in the size of the DOM tree. It relies on finding structurally identical subtrees, not just similarly-tagged nodes.

key_deltas.summary: Abandons record-level similarity comparison in favor of a bottom-up reconstruction process that starts from small, stable 'invariant subtrees' and grows upwards to find the full record boundaries.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The approach depends on the existence of at least one common, non-trivial invariant subtree across records. It also introduces hyperparameters for pattern mining, such as minimum frequency and length thresholds, which may need tuning.

Ig.name: Invariant-Guided
Ig.mechanism_trigger: The `Encode_STR` function, which computes a node's code based only on its own subtree.
Ig.why_it_applies: The design intent of `Encode_STR` is to create a representation for a subtree that is invariant to its position (depth) within the larger DOM tree. This directly addresses the problem of nested records, where semantically identical subtrees appear at different depths.

Ig.name: Invariant-Guided
Ig.mechanism_trigger: The entire two-stage process of finding 'invariant subtrees' via frequent pattern mining and then matching 'invariant paths' upwards.
Ig.why_it_applies: The paper's central thesis is to replace brittle similarity assumptions with the concept of 'record invariants'. The algorithm is explicitly designed to find and exploit these invariants to reconstruct records, making it the core guiding principle of the design.

Lu.name: Language Homogeneity
Lu.mechanism_trigger: The transformation of the DOM tree into a Node Encoding Sequence (NES).
Lu.why_it_applies: The paper explicitly flattens the tree structure into a sequence to leverage powerful and efficient algorithms from sequential pattern mining (e.g., using suffix trees). This transformation into a homogeneous representation is a key enabler of the overall approach.

Mo.name: Modularity
Mo.mechanism_trigger: The recursive definition of `Encode_STR`.
Mo.why_it_applies: The encoding of a subtree is modular; it depends only on its own content and the encodings of its children, not on its parent or siblings. This self-contained, compositional definition is what enables the position-invariance.

Gr.name: Generalized Design
Gr.mechanism_trigger: The reformulation of the problem assumptions from 'uniform schema and linear structure' to 'existence of invariant subtrees and paths'.
Gr.why_it_applies: The paper explicitly generalizes the problem definition to cover both old (Web 1.0) and modern (Web 2.0) pages. The proposed mechanism works for the general case, of which the baseline's assumptions are a specialized subset. This allows Miria to perform well on both types of datasets.

vldb2023-122.paper_title: Distributed Graph Embedding with Information-Oriented Random Walks
vldb2023-122.goal: To present a general-purpose, distributed, information-centric random walk-based graph embedding framework, DistGER, which can scale to embed billion-edge graphs.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Acceleration compared to state-of-the-art distributed graph embedding frameworks (KnightKing, DistDGL, PytorchBigGraph).
Average reduction in cross-machine communication compared to KnightKing's partitioning.
Effectiveness improvement (AUC score) in downstream tasks compared to competitors.

vldb2023-122.limiting_design_assumption: On-the-fly effectiveness measurement of a random walk in a distributed setting requires sending the full walk path in messages, leading to computation and communication costs that are at least linear in the walk length.
vldb2023-122.primary_tool_specific_type: Distributed random walk and embedding training system
vldb2023-122.state_or_ir: Random walks (sequences of nodes), node embeddings (vectors), local frequency lists, and intermediate statistics for entropy calculation (H, L, E(H), etc.).
vldb2023-122.output: Low-dimensional vector embeddings for each node in the input graph.
vldb2023-122.equation: Full-path distributed walk computation + Rc + Wv + Lo -> DistGER
vldb2023-122.principle_composition_rationale: The composition transforms a naive distributed implementation into an efficient one by first changing the core algorithm to be incremental and communication-light (Rc, Wv), and then optimizing the placement of data and computation to align with the workload's access patterns (Lo).
vldb2023-122.one_sentence_mechanism: DistGER accelerates distributed graph embedding by replacing the expensive, full-path-based computation of information-theoretic walk termination criteria with an O(1) incremental update, and by co-locating data and computation through proximity-aware partitioning and locality-aware training.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces full-path computation for walk effectiveness with an O(1) incremental update, reducing both computation and communication overhead.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Each machine must maintain local state (frequency lists) for ongoing walks. Messages are constant-sized but larger than in systems like KnightKing that do not perform information-centric measurements.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a locality-aware graph partitioner (MPGP) to reduce cross-machine walks and a specialized distributed Skip-Gram learner (DSGL) that improves data locality and CPU throughput during training.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: MPGP partitioning is more computationally expensive than simple load balancing. DSGL adds implementation complexity with local buffers and a specific synchronization protocol.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The incremental computation of walk information entropy (H) and coefficient of determination (R^2).
Rc.why_it_applies: The system avoids recomputing statistics over the entire walk path at each step by deriving and using recurrence relations that update the statistics from step L to L+1 in O(1) time, reusing the results from the previous step.

Wv.name: Work Avoidance
Wv.mechanism_trigger: Sending constant-size messages with statistical aggregates instead of the full walk path.
Wv.why_it_applies: The incremental computation proves that the full path information is not needed to make the walk termination decision. By sending only the necessary aggregates, the system avoids the work of serializing, sending, and deserializing the full path, without changing the externally observable termination decision.

Si.name: Simplicity
Si.mechanism_trigger: The design of a constant-size message format for cross-machine communication during walks.
Si.why_it_applies: By ensuring all messages have a fixed, constant size (80 bytes), the communication protocol and performance analysis are simplified compared to a baseline with variable-length messages that depend on walk length.

Lo.name: Locality of Reference
Lo.mechanism_trigger: The MPGP partitioning scheme and the DSGL training model's use of local buffers and frequency-sorted global matrices.
Lo.why_it_applies: MPGP explicitly partitions the graph to co-locate nodes with high proximity, increasing the chance a walk remains on one machine. DSGL uses thread-local buffers to avoid cross-core cache coherency traffic (cache-line ping-ponging) and sorts global matrices by frequency to improve cache locality for frequently accessed nodes.

Cc.name: Common-Case Specialization
Cc.mechanism_trigger: The hotness-block based synchronization mechanism in DSGL.
Cc.why_it_applies: The mechanism identifies high-frequency ('hot') nodes as the common case for updates and synchronizes their vectors more frequently than low-frequency nodes. This optimizes for the skewed access pattern typical in graph workloads.

Wa.name: Workload-Aware
Wa.mechanism_trigger: The objective function of the MPGP partitioner.
Wa.why_it_applies: The partitioner's objective function uses first and second-order proximity scores, which are chosen specifically because they align with the behavior of random walks. The partitioning is thus aware of the access patterns of its primary workload.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: The multi-windows shared-negative samples mechanism in DSGL.
Ha.why_it_applies: This mechanism batches updates from multiple context windows to create larger matrix operations. This is explicitly done to convert memory-bandwidth-limited Level-1 BLAS operations into more efficient, compute-bound Level-3 BLAS operations, which better utilizes modern CPU throughput.

vldb2023-123.paper_title: Estimating Single-Node PageRank in 𝑂 ˜ (︁ min { 𝑑𝑡 , √ 𝑚 } )︁ Time
vldb2023-123.problem_domain: Single-node PageRank computation on undirected graphs
vldb2023-123.goal: To efficiently estimate the PageRank score of a single target node on large-scale undirected graphs with a sublinear time complexity.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Query time complexity for estimating a target node t's PageRank with constant relative error and constant failure probability on undirected graphs.
Improvement in query time over existing methods for low-degree query nodes.

vldb2023-123.baseline_name: Reverse exploration methods (e.g., LocalPush) and Monte-Carlo methods
vldb2023-123.limiting_design_assumption: Estimating single-node PageRank requires either (1) simulating a large number of random walks (Ω(n) in the worst case) to hit the target node, or (2) deterministically exploring all neighbors in a reverse push (Ω(d_u) cost per step), which can be Ω(n) for high-degree nodes.
vldb2023-123.state_or_ir: ℓ-hop residue vectors (r^(ℓ)_t) for each hop ℓ up to a truncation limit L.
vldb2023-123.output: An estimate of the target node's PageRank, ˆπ(t).
vldb2023-123.equation: Global PageRank via Reverse Exploration + Ep + Pd + Cc -> SetPush
vldb2023-123.principle_composition_rationale: The composition transforms an expensive global computation into a tractable local one. Ep provides the mathematical equivalence to rewrite the global PageRank problem as a local backward exploration from the target node. This local exploration, however, is still bottlenecked by high-degree nodes. Pd and Cc work together to solve this bottleneck: Cc creates a two-path execution based on a cost threshold, and Pd provides the low-cost, randomized sampling path that avoids deterministically visiting all neighbors of a high-degree node.
vldb2023-123.one_sentence_mechanism: SetPush achieves sublinear single-node PageRank estimation by reformulating the problem as a local backward exploration using graph reversibility properties, and then making this exploration efficient via a novel hybrid push operation that switches between deterministic and probabilistic propagation based on a cost threshold.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces the deterministic backward push with a hybrid operation that chooses between a full deterministic push and a randomized partial push based on a cost threshold, avoiding the O(d_u) cost for high-degree nodes.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The estimation becomes probabilistic, introducing variance that must be theoretically bounded to guarantee correctness. The algorithm is randomized rather than deterministic.

key_deltas.summary: Reformulates the global PageRank estimation problem as computing a local, truncated sum of l-hop Personalized PageRank (PPR) scores, using the reversibility property of PPR on undirected graphs to enable efficient backward exploration from the target node.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The estimate is for a truncated PageRank, which introduces a small, bounded approximation error relative to the true PageRank. The approach is specific to undirected graphs where the reversibility property holds.

Pd.name: Probabilistic Design
Pd.mechanism_trigger: The use of geometric sampling to select a subset of neighbors to push probability mass to when the deterministic push would be too expensive.
Pd.why_it_applies: The algorithm uses a randomized process (geometric sampling) to estimate the result of a deterministic operation (pushing to all neighbors) to improve performance. The estimate is unbiased in expectation.

Cc.name: Common-Case Specialization
Cc.mechanism_trigger: The `if (1-α)·r(ℓ)t(u) ≥ θ·d_u` condition which selects between two different implementations of the push operation.
Cc.why_it_applies: The algorithm has two distinct execution paths for the push operation, specializing the implementation based on a condition that separates high-cost cases (small residue on a high-degree node) from low-cost ones.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The design of the hybrid push operation specifically to avoid the O(d_u) cost of pushing from high-degree nodes.
Bo.why_it_applies: The paper explicitly identifies the cost of reverse push from high-degree nodes as a performance bottleneck for prior methods, and the hybrid push mechanism is designed to mitigate this specific bottleneck.

Ep.name: Equivalence-based
Ep.mechanism_trigger: The use of the PPR reversibility property, π^(l)_s(t)·d_s = π^(l)_t(s)·d_t, to transform the all-source PageRank computation into a single-target backward computation.
Ep.why_it_applies: The algorithm is founded on a mathematical equivalence that reformulates the problem from a global computation (averaging over all n nodes) into a local one (exploring from one node), which is more efficient to solve.

Cr.name: Consistency Relaxation
Cr.mechanism_trigger: The decision to compute a 'truncated PageRank' (summing up to L hops) instead of the full PageRank (infinite sum).
Cr.why_it_applies: The algorithm deliberately computes an approximation of the true value by truncating an infinite series. The paper proves this relaxation introduces a bounded relative error, trading perfect accuracy for tractability.

Lo.name: Locality of Reference
Lo.mechanism_trigger: The transformation from an all-source problem to a single-target backward exploration.
Lo.why_it_applies: The reformulation allows the algorithm to be 'local', exploring only a small fraction of the graph around the target node instead of requiring global information or walks from all nodes. This improves computational locality.

vldb2023-124.paper_title: Space-Efficient Random Walks on Streaming Graphs
vldb2023-124.problem_domain: Maintaining random walks on streaming graphs
vldb2023-124.goal: To efficiently store and update random walks on streaming graphs to avoid stale walks and performance degradation in downstream tasks.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Throughput improvement over an inverted index-based baseline on real-world graphs.
Latency improvement over an inverted index-based baseline on real-world graphs.
Memory footprint reduction compared to an inverted index-based baseline.

vldb2023-124.limiting_design_assumption: Storing random walks as raw sequences of vertices, separate from the graph, and using a simple inverted index to map vertices to the walks they participate in is an efficient method for finding and updating affected walks.
vldb2023-124.state_or_ir: A hybrid-tree data structure where the graph and walk corpus are stored together. Walks are represented as a collection of integer-encoded triplets of the form (walk_id, position, next_vertex_id).
vldb2023-124.output: An updated, statistically indistinguishable walk corpus after a batch of graph updates.
vldb2023-124.equation: Inverted Index on Raw Walk Sequences + Se + Wv + Ha -> Wharf
vldb2023-124.principle_composition_rationale: This composition transforms an inefficient search-and-update problem into an efficient one. By making the walk structure Semantically Explicit (Se) as triplets, the system can define a mathematical ordering. This ordering enables Work Avoidance (Wv) via range-pruned search. The entire representation is then implemented in a Hardware-Aware (Ha) data structure (C-trees) to ensure cache locality and space efficiency.
vldb2023-124.one_sentence_mechanism: Wharf maintains random walks on streaming graphs by representing them as integer-encoded triplets stored in a compressed, hardware-aware tree, which enables provable search space pruning and efficient batch updates.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces raw walk sequences and a separate inverted index with a unified hybrid-tree where walks are stored as compressed, encoded integer triplets, enabling both space savings and efficient indexed access.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Adds computational overhead for encoding and decoding triplets during updates, though this was measured to be a small fraction (3-13%) of the total update time.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces an output-sensitive search algorithm and parallel batch updates, leveraging the ordered, encoded walk representation to efficiently find and modify affected walk segments.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The on-demand merge policy can increase memory footprint between merges to achieve higher throughput. The update logic is more complex than the baseline's sequential approach.

Se.name: Semantically Explicit
Se.mechanism_trigger: The representation of a walk is changed from an opaque sequence of vertex IDs to a collection of explicit triplets: `(walk_id, position, next_vertex_id)`.
Se.why_it_applies: This mechanism makes the structure of the walk corpus explicit and queryable at the granularity of a single step. The system can now directly access and reason about a vertex at a specific position in a specific walk, which is the foundation for efficient updates.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: The use of Compressed Purely-Functional Trees (C-trees) with chunking and difference encoding to store the encoded walk triplets.
Ha.why_it_applies: The paper explicitly states that C-trees are chosen to improve space consumption and cache performance. The chunking mechanism is designed for cache locality.

Gr.name: Generalized Design
Gr.mechanism_trigger: The walk update algorithm is designed to be independent of the specific random walk model (e.g., DeepWalk, node2vec).
Gr.why_it_applies: The paper notes that the update policy is orthogonal to the algorithm, allowing different random walk models to be plugged in, which demonstrates a separation of mechanism (the data structure and update logic) from policy (the walk sampling strategy).

Wv.name: Work Avoidance: skip work that cannot alter externally observable results
Wv.mechanism_trigger: The `FindNext` algorithm uses the ordering property of the pairing function to construct a search range `[low, high]` for a given `(walk_id, position)` query.
Wv.why_it_applies: This range allows the search to provably skip traversing and decoding entire subtrees and chunks of the walk-tree that cannot possibly contain the target triplet. The skipped work is guaranteed not to affect the correctness of the search result.

Cz.name: Coordination Avoidance
Cz.mechanism_trigger: The use of batching (via an 'insertion accumulator') and purely-functional data structures (PAM, C-trees) that support safe parallelism.
Cz.why_it_applies: The system collects updates and applies them in a single, parallel `MultiInsert` operation. The underlying data structures are designed to minimize coordination and enable concurrent operations, which is a form of coordination avoidance.

vldb2023-125.problem_domain: Graph mining and social network analysis
vldb2023-125.goal: To find a group of users in a social network that maximizes total vertex weight, subject to personalized upper and lower bounds on each user's local neighborhood density within the group.

concrete_numbers.meaning: The objective ratio of the proposed FSGSel-Hybrid algorithm compared to the optimal IP solution on the LiveJ dataset.
The feasibility ratio achieved by the FSGSel-TD algorithm, meaning all vertices in the returned subgraph satisfy their personalized local density requirements.

vldb2023-125.baseline_name: Existing dense and sparse subgraph/group queries
vldb2023-125.limiting_design_assumption: Existing queries assume a unified, global lower bound for density (e.g., k-core) for all members of a group and cannot support personalized density requirements, especially both upper and lower bounds, for individual vertices.
vldb2023-125.primary_tool_specific_type: Integer Program Relaxation and Dynamic Programming on Tree Decompositions
vldb2023-125.state_or_ir: A graph G=(V,E), vertex weights, edge weights, and per-vertex density constraints.
vldb2023-125.output: A subgraph H* that maximizes total vertex weight while satisfying all constraints.
vldb2023-125.equation: Unified Density Queries + Cp + Ep -> DCSGQ
vldb2023-125.principle_composition_rationale: This composition transforms a rigid group query problem into a flexible, solvable one. Cp generalizes the problem definition to capture richer, personalized requirements. This increased complexity makes the problem NP-hard, which is then made tractable for a common class of inputs (low-treewidth graphs) by using Ep to systematically decompose the problem into solvable subproblems based on the graph's structure.
vldb2023-125.one_sentence_mechanism: Introduces personalized upper and lower bound density constraints for each vertex in a group query and solves the resulting NP-hard problem using an integer program relaxation and a fixed-parameter tractable algorithm based on tree decomposition for graphs with low treewidth.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a new query (DCSGQ) that supports personalized, per-vertex local density constraints, including both a lower bound (for social tightness) and an upper bound (for social sparseness).
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The resulting problem (DCSGQ) is NP-hard and inapproximable within any ratio, making it computationally challenging.

key_deltas.summary: Develops a fixed-parameter tractable (FPT) algorithm, FSGSel-TD, that solves DCSGQ with a performance guarantee by leveraging the tree-like structure of many real-world graphs.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The algorithm's time complexity is exponential in the treewidth of the graph, making it potentially slow for dense graphs with high treewidth.

Cp.name: Constraint-based
Cp.mechanism_trigger: The formulation of the DCSGQ problem itself, which is defined by a set of personalized local density constraints for each vertex.
Cp.why_it_applies: The core novelty of the problem is transforming the search for a 'dense' group into a search for a group that satisfies a collection of user-specified constraints on local structure, `d(u) >= d_H*(u) >= d(u)`.

Gr.name: Generalized Design
Gr.mechanism_trigger: The use of both an upper and a lower bound for the density constraint.
Gr.why_it_applies: The paper explicitly states that the personalized density range is a more general form that can subsume traditional dense or sparse queries by setting one of the bounds to an extreme value (0 or infinity).

Ep.name: Equivalence-based
Ep.mechanism_trigger: The FSGSel-TD algorithm, which uses dynamic programming on a tree decomposition of the graph.
Ep.why_it_applies: The algorithm breaks the main problem into a series of smaller, equivalent subproblems defined on the bags of the tree decomposition. It solves these subproblems recursively and combines their solutions based on the bag types (Leaf, Introduce, Forget, Join) to find the global optimum for the original problem.

Cc.name: Common-Case Specialization
Cc.mechanism_trigger: The choice to use a tree decomposition-based algorithm and the design of the hybrid approach.
Cc.why_it_applies: The entire motivation for the FSGSel-TD algorithm is that the common case for social networks is low treewidth. The FSGSel-Hybrid algorithm makes this explicit by partitioning the graph into a dense 'core' and low-treewidth 'fringes' and applying different algorithms to each, specializing the performant-but-complex TD algorithm for the common fringe structure.

vldb2023-126.paper_title: MiniGraph: Querying Big Graphs with a Single Machine
vldb2023-126.goal: To systematically reduce the I/O cost of an out-of-core graph system and improve multi-core parallelism on a single machine.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Faster than prior out-of-core systems on WCC over the datagen graph.
Disk read for WCC on web-sk graph for MiniGraph vs. GridGraph.
Number of supersteps for WCC on web-sk graph for MiniGraph vs. GridGraph.
MiniGraph on a single machine performs better than some multi-machine systems that use up to 12 machines.

vldb2023-126.limiting_design_assumption: The vertex-centric (VC) model is sufficient for parallel graph computation. This assumption leads to excessive I/O, especially for graphs with large diameters, because it is inefficient for 'beyond-neighborhood' computations and requires many supersteps.
vldb2023-126.state_or_ir: Graph partitions (subgraphs), worker states (e.g., Active, Converged), and an in-memory MessageStore for updates.
vldb2023-126.output: The results of graph algorithms like WCC, SSSP, and PageRank.
vldb2023-126.equation: Vertex-centric out-of-core systems + Bo + Gr + Ha -> MiniGraph
vldb2023-126.principle_composition_rationale: The composition addresses the primary limitations of vertex-centric systems by tackling both the I/O bottleneck and the restrictive programming model. Bo and Ha work together to create a pipelined architecture that hides I/O latency by making disk access patterns hardware-friendly. This efficient I/O pipeline enables the Gr (Generalized Design) of the hybrid parallel model to be effective, as it can now efficiently load and process entire subgraphs required by the graph-centric part of the model without being constantly stalled by I/O.
vldb2023-126.one_sentence_mechanism: MiniGraph reduces I/O and improves multi-core utilization in single-machine graph processing by using a pipelined architecture to overlap I/O and CPU, and a hybrid graph-centric/vertex-centric model to enable efficient two-level parallelism.

key_deltas.summary: Introduces a three-stage pipelined architecture (Loader, Evaluator, Discharger) to overlap I/O operations with CPU computations, mitigating the I/O bottleneck and improving CPU utilization.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Increased system complexity due to asynchronous components and the need for a scheduler to keep the pipeline full.

key_deltas.summary: Implements a hybrid parallel model that combines the graph-centric (GC) model for inter-subgraph parallelism with the vertex-centric (VC) model for intra-subgraph parallelism, enabling more efficient algorithms and better multi-core utilization.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: A more complex programming model (PIE+) and a new, NP-complete resource scheduling problem to balance the two levels of parallelism.

Bo.name: Bottleneck-Oriented
Bo.mechanism_trigger: The design of the entire pipelined architecture.
Bo.why_it_applies: The paper explicitly identifies disk I/O as 'the bottleneck of the singlemachine systems' and the pipelined architecture is designed to 'overlap I/O and CPU operations, so as to 'cancel' the excessive I/O cost'.

Ha.name: Hardware-Aware
Ha.mechanism_trigger: The Loader and Discharger components' I/O strategy.
Ha.why_it_applies: The system is designed to 'promote sequential accesses to the disk' and the Loader 'issues a small number of bulk read requests'. This is aware of the performance characteristics of secondary storage like SSDs and HDDs, where sequential I/O is much faster than random I/O.

Mo.name: Modularity
Mo.mechanism_trigger: The decomposition into Loader, Evaluator, and Discharger.
Mo.why_it_applies: The architecture is decomposed into three loosely coupled components communicating via queues. This 'decouples computation from memory management and scheduling, giving rises to new opportunities for optimizations'.

Gr.name: Generalized Design
Gr.mechanism_trigger: The creation of the hybrid parallel model and the PIE+ API.
Gr.why_it_applies: MiniGraph creates a 'hybrid model, supporting both the data-partitioned parallelism of GC and the operation-level parallelism of VC' through a 'unified interface'. This generalizes beyond single-model systems, allowing users to 'choose one that fits their applications and graphs the best'.

Rc.name: Reuse of Computation
Rc.mechanism_trigger: The IncEval step in the Graph-Centric (GC) model.
Rc.why_it_applies: The GC model, which MiniGraph adopts, is based on incremental computation. The IncEval step is 'repeatedly executed to refine the partial results by treating messages from other workers as updates', which reuses the previously computed state instead of recomputing from scratch in each superstep.

Wa.name: Workload-Aware
Wa.mechanism_trigger: The resource scheduler's use of a cost model.
Wa.why_it_applies: The scheduler uses a cost function `C_A` to estimate execution time, which is trained on 'historic running logs' and depends on the specific algorithm `A` and graph structural properties. This makes scheduling decisions aware of the specific workload.

vldb2023-127.paper_title: Parallel Colorful h-star Core Maintenance in Dynamic Graphs
vldb2023-127.problem_domain: Cohesive subgraph mining in large-scale static and dynamic graphs
vldb2023-127.goal: To develop a highly parallelizable and efficient algorithm for colorful h-star core decomposition and maintenance, overcoming the low parallelism and inefficiency of existing sequential peeling algorithms, especially for dynamic graphs.

concrete_numbers.meaning: Faster convergence speed compared to baselines for static decomposition.
Speedup for handling graph changes (updates) compared to recomputation.
Speedup for the parallel local algorithm with 64 threads compared to its sequential version.
Speedup of the EdgeIns algorithm for a single edge insertion compared to recomputation.

vldb2023-127.limiting_design_assumption: Core decomposition requires global information (the minimum colorful h-star degree in the entire remaining graph) and destructive, iterative graph modifications (peeling nodes). This design is inherently sequential and inefficient for dynamic updates.
vldb2023-127.state_or_ir: The n-order H-index for each node, which serves as a monotonically decreasing upper bound on the final core number.
vldb2023-127.output: The converged colorful h-star core number for every node in the graph.
vldb2023-127.equation: Global Peeling + Dc + Cz + Wv -> Local, Dynamic Core Maintenance
vldb2023-127.principle_composition_rationale: Dc and Cz work in tandem to transform the static decomposition from a sequential, centralized process into a massively parallel, local one. Wv then leverages the local nature of this new formulation to create an efficient dynamic maintenance algorithm by proving which nodes are unaffected by updates, thus avoiding the bottleneck of full recomputation.
vldb2023-127.one_sentence_mechanism: Replaces a global, sequential peeling algorithm with a highly parallel, local iterative algorithm based on an 'n-order H-index', and uses theoretical bounds to avoid recomputation for unaffected nodes during dynamic graph updates.

key_deltas.summary: Replaces the global, sequential peeling algorithm with a local, iterative algorithm where each node computes its core number based only on information from its neighbors, enabling high parallelism.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: The local algorithm requires multiple iterations to converge, whereas the peeling algorithm is a single (though sequential) pass. The total work might be higher if not executed in parallel.

key_deltas.summary: For dynamic graphs, avoids full recomputation by first identifying a small subset of potentially affected nodes using derived theoretical bounds, and then running the local update algorithm only on this subset.
key_deltas.tradeoffs_or_costs: Adds the overhead of computing bounds and performing a bounded graph traversal (BFS) to identify the affected set. This is a beneficial tradeoff as the affected set is typically very small.

Dc.name: Decentralized Control
Dc.mechanism_trigger: The replacement of the global minimum degree search in the peeling algorithm with the n-order H-index computation, where each node updates its state based only on its local neighborhood.
